title: Bob Dylan

infobox.value: Robert Allen Zimmerman   (1941-05-24) May 24, 1941 (age 84)  [Duluth, Minnesota](/wiki/Duluth,_Minnesota "Duluth, Minnesota"), U.S.

* Shabtai Zisel ben Avraham ([Hebrew name](/wiki/Hebrew_name "Hebrew name")) * Elston Gunnn * Blind Boy Grunt * Bob Landy * Robert Milkwood Thomas * Tedham Porterhouse * [Lucky Wilbury](/wiki/Traveling_Wilburys "Traveling Wilburys") * [Boo Wilbury](/wiki/Traveling_Wilburys "Traveling Wilburys") * Jack Frost * Sergei Petrov * Zimmy
[Sara Lownds](/wiki/Sara_Lownds "Sara Lownds")  ​  ​ (m. 1965; div. 1977)​      [Carolyn Dennis](/wiki/Carolyn_Dennis "Carolyn Dennis")  ​  ​ (m. 1986; div. 1992)​

* [2016 Nobel Prize in Literature](/wiki/2016_Nobel_Prize_in_Literature "2016 Nobel Prize in Literature") * (for others, see [list](/wiki/List_of_awards_and_nominations_received_by_Bob_Dylan "List of awards and nominations received by Bob Dylan"))

* [Folk](/wiki/Contemporary_folk_music "Contemporary folk music") * [blues](/wiki/Blues "Blues") * [rock](/wiki/Rock_music "Rock music") * [gospel](/wiki/Gospel_music "Gospel music") * [country](/wiki/Country_music "Country music") * [traditional pop](/wiki/Traditional_pop "Traditional pop")

* [Albums and singles](/wiki/Bob_Dylan_discography "Bob Dylan discography") * [songs](/wiki/List_of_songs_written_by_Bob_Dylan "List of songs written by Bob Dylan") * [bootleg recordings](/wiki/Bob_Dylan_bootleg_recordings "Bob Dylan bootleg recordings")

(legally **Robert Dylan**;[[1]](#cite_note-1) born **Robert Allen Zimmerman**, May 24, 1941) is an American singer-songwriter. Described as one of the greatest songwriters of all time, Dylan has been a major figure in popular culture over his 69-year career. With an estimated 125 million records sold worldwide,[[2]](#cite_note-2) he is one of the [best-selling musicians](/wiki/List_of_best-selling_music_artists "List of best-selling music artists").[[3]](#cite_note-3) Dylan added increasingly sophisticated lyrical techniques to the [folk music](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music") of the early 1960s, infusing it "with the intellectualism of classic literature and poetry".[[4]](#cite_note-EncBr-4) His lyrics incorporated political, social, and philosophical influences, defying [pop music](/wiki/Pop_music "Pop music") conventions and appealing to the burgeoning [counterculture](/wiki/Counterculture_of_the_1960s "Counterculture of the 1960s").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American singer-songwriter (born 1941)

This article is about the musician. For his debut album, see [*Bob Dylan* (album)](/wiki/Bob_Dylan_(album) "Bob Dylan (album)").

**Bob Dylan** (legally **Robert Dylan**;[[1]](#cite_note-1) born **Robert Allen Zimmerman**, May 24, 1941) is an American singer-songwriter. Described as one of the greatest songwriters of all time, Dylan has been a major figure in popular culture over his 69-year career. With an estimated 125 million records sold worldwide,[[2]](#cite_note-2) he is one of the [best-selling musicians](/wiki/List_of_best-selling_music_artists "List of best-selling music artists").[[3]](#cite_note-3) Dylan added increasingly sophisticated lyrical techniques to the [folk music](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music") of the early 1960s, infusing it "with the intellectualism of classic literature and poetry".[[4]](#cite_note-EncBr-4) His lyrics incorporated political, social, and philosophical influences, defying [pop music](/wiki/Pop_music "Pop music") conventions and appealing to the burgeoning [counterculture](/wiki/Counterculture_of_the_1960s "Counterculture of the 1960s").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Dylan was born in [St. Louis County, Minnesota](/wiki/St._Louis_County,_Minnesota "St. Louis County, Minnesota"). He moved to New York City in 1961 to pursue a music career. His 1962 debut album, *[Bob Dylan](/wiki/Bob_Dylan_(album) "Bob Dylan (album)")*, containing traditional folk and blues material, was followed by his breakthrough album, *[The Freewheelin' Bob Dylan](/wiki/The_Freewheelin%27_Bob_Dylan "The Freewheelin' Bob Dylan")* (1963), which included "[Girl from the North Country](/wiki/Girl_from_the_North_Country "Girl from the North Country")" and "[A Hard Rain's a-Gonna Fall](/wiki/A_Hard_Rain%27s_a-Gonna_Fall "A Hard Rain's a-Gonna Fall")", adapting older folk songs. His songs "[Blowin' in the Wind](/wiki/Blowin%27_in_the_Wind "Blowin' in the Wind")" (1963) and "[The Times They Are a-Changin'](/wiki/The_Times_They_Are_a-Changin%27_(song) "The Times They Are a-Changin' (song)")" (1964) became anthems for the [civil rights](/wiki/Civil_rights_movement "Civil rights movement") and [antiwar](/wiki/Opposition_to_United_States_involvement_in_the_Vietnam_War "Opposition to United States involvement in the Vietnam War") movements. In 1965 and 1966, Dylan [created controversy](/wiki/Electric_Dylan_controversy "Electric Dylan controversy") when he used [electrically amplified](/wiki/Amplifier "Amplifier") rock instrumentation for his albums *[Bringing It All Back Home](/wiki/Bringing_It_All_Back_Home "Bringing It All Back Home")*, *[Highway 61 Revisited](/wiki/Highway_61_Revisited "Highway 61 Revisited")* (both 1965), and *[Blonde on Blonde](/wiki/Blonde_on_Blonde "Blonde on Blonde")* (1966). His six-minute single "[Like a Rolling Stone](/wiki/Like_a_Rolling_Stone "Like a Rolling Stone")" (1965) expanded commercial and creative boundaries in popular music.

After a motorcycle crash in 1966, Dylan ceased touring for seven years. During this period, he recorded [a large body of songs](/wiki/List_of_Basement_Tapes_songs "List of Basement Tapes songs") with members of [the Band](/wiki/The_Band "The Band"), which produced the album *[The Basement Tapes](/wiki/The_Basement_Tapes "The Basement Tapes")* (1975). Dylan explored [country music](/wiki/Country_music "Country music") and rural themes on the albums *[John Wesley Harding](/wiki/John_Wesley_Harding "John Wesley Harding")* (1967), *[Nashville Skyline](/wiki/Nashville_Skyline "Nashville Skyline")* (1969), and *[New Morning](/wiki/New_Morning "New Morning")* (1970). He gained acclaim for *[Blood on the Tracks](/wiki/Blood_on_the_Tracks "Blood on the Tracks")* (1975) and *[Time Out of Mind](/wiki/Time_Out_of_Mind_(Bob_Dylan_album) "Time Out of Mind (Bob Dylan album)")* (1997), the latter of which earned him the [Grammy Award for Album of the Year](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Album_of_the_Year "Grammy Award for Album of the Year"). Dylan still releases music and has toured continually since the late 1980s on what has become known as the [Never Ending Tour](/wiki/Never_Ending_Tour "Never Ending Tour"). Since 1994, Dylan has published [ten books of paintings and drawings](/wiki/Bob_Dylan_bibliography#Art_books_by_Bob_Dylan "Bob Dylan bibliography"), and his work has been exhibited in major art galleries. His life has been profiled in several films, including the biopic *[A Complete Unknown](/wiki/A_Complete_Unknown "A Complete Unknown")* (2024).

[Dylan's accolades](/wiki/List_of_awards_received_by_Bob_Dylan "List of awards received by Bob Dylan") include an [Academy Award](/wiki/Academy_Award "Academy Award") and ten [Grammy Awards](/wiki/Grammy_Award "Grammy Award"). He was honored with [Kennedy Center Honors](/wiki/Kennedy_Center_Honors "Kennedy Center Honors") in 1997 and the [Presidential Medal of Freedom](/wiki/Presidential_Medal_of_Freedom "Presidential Medal of Freedom") in 2012. Dylan has been inducted into the [Rock and Roll Hall of Fame](/wiki/Rock_and_Roll_Hall_of_Fame "Rock and Roll Hall of Fame") and the [Songwriters Hall of Fame](/wiki/Songwriters_Hall_of_Fame "Songwriters Hall of Fame"). He was awarded a [Pulitzer Prize special citation](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize_Special_Citations_and_Awards "Pulitzer Prize Special Citations and Awards") in 2008, and the 2016 [Nobel Prize in Literature](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Literature "Nobel Prize in Literature") "for having created new poetic expressions within the great American song tradition".[[6]](#cite_note-NYTNobel-6)

## Early life and education

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/ZimmermanHouseHibbingMN.jpg/250px-ZimmermanHouseHibbingMN.jpg)](/wiki/File:ZimmermanHouseHibbingMN.jpg)

The Zimmerman family home in Hibbing, Minnesota

Bob Dylan was born Robert Allen Zimmerman (his [Hebrew name](/wiki/Hebrew_name "Hebrew name") is *Shabtai Zisl ben Avraham*)[[7]](#cite_note-Sounes14-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9) on May 24, 1941, at St. Mary's Hospital, in [Duluth, Minnesota](/wiki/Duluth,_Minnesota "Duluth, Minnesota").[[10]](#cite_note-10) Dylan's paternal grandparents, Anna Kirghiz and Zigman Zimmerman, emigrated from [Odessa](/wiki/Odessa "Odessa") in the [Russian Empire](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire") (now Ukraine) to the United States after the [1905 pogroms against Jews](/wiki/Odessa_pogroms#1905_pogrom "Odessa pogroms"). His maternal grandparents, Florence and Ben Stone, were [Lithuanian Jews](/wiki/Lithuanian_Jews "Lithuanian Jews") who had arrived in the US in 1902.[[11]](#cite_note-Sounes-p12-11) Dylan wrote that his paternal grandmother's family was originally from the [Kağızman District](/wiki/Ka%C4%9F%C4%B1zman_District "Kağızman District") of [Kars Province](/wiki/Kars_Province "Kars Province"), northeastern Turkey.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

Dylan's father Abram Zimmerman and his mother Beatrice "Beatty" Stone were part of a small, close-knit Jewish community.[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-Haaretz-15) They lived in Duluth until Dylan was six, when his father contracted [polio](/wiki/Polio "Polio") and the family returned to his mother's hometown of [Hibbing](/wiki/Hibbing "Hibbing"), where they lived for the rest of Dylan's childhood, and his father and paternal uncles ran a furniture and appliance store.[[15]](#cite_note-Haaretz-15)[[16]](#cite_note-Hibb-16)

In the early 1950s, Dylan listened to the *[Grand Ole Opry](/wiki/Grand_Ole_Opry "Grand Ole Opry")* radio show and heard the songs of [Hank Williams](/wiki/Hank_Williams "Hank Williams"). He later wrote: "The sound of his voice went through me like an electric rod."[[17]](#cite_note-chronicles95-17) Dylan was also impressed by the delivery of [Johnnie Ray](/wiki/Johnnie_Ray "Johnnie Ray"): "He was the first singer whose voice and style, I guess, I totally fell in love with.... I loved his style, wanted to dress like him too."[[18]](#cite_note-18) As a teenager, Dylan heard [rock and roll](/wiki/Rock_and_roll "Rock and roll") on radio stations broadcasting from [Shreveport](/wiki/Shreveport "Shreveport") and [Little Rock](/wiki/Little_Rock "Little Rock").[[19]](#cite_note-Shelton-38-19)

Dylan formed several bands while attending [Hibbing High School](/wiki/Hibbing_High_School "Hibbing High School"). In the Golden Chords, he performed [covers](/wiki/Cover_version "Cover version") of songs by [Little Richard](/wiki/Little_Richard "Little Richard")[[20]](#cite_note-GrayJapan-20) and [Elvis Presley](/wiki/Elvis_Presley "Elvis Presley").[[21]](#cite_note-21) Their performance of [Danny & the Juniors](/wiki/Danny_%26_the_Juniors "Danny & the Juniors")' "[Rock and Roll Is Here to Stay](/wiki/Rock_and_Roll_Is_Here_to_Stay "Rock and Roll Is Here to Stay")" at their high school talent show was so loud that the principal cut the microphone.[[22]](#cite_note-22) On January 31, 1959, 17-year-old Dylan saw [Buddy Holly](/wiki/Buddy_Holly "Buddy Holly") perform at the [Duluth Armory](/wiki/Duluth_Armory "Duluth Armory"), four days before [Holly's fatal plane crash](/wiki/The_Day_the_Music_Died "The Day the Music Died"). Dylan was electrified, and in his Nobel Prize lecture, he explained: "Buddy wrote songs – songs that had beautiful melodies and imaginative verses. And he sang great – sang in more than a few voices. He was the archetype. Everything I wasn't and wanted to be."[[23]](#cite_note-23)

In 1959, Dylan's high school yearbook carried the caption "Robert Zimmerman: to join 'Little Richard'".[[20]](#cite_note-GrayJapan-20)[[24]](#cite_note-24) That year, as Elston Gunnn, he performed twice with [Bobby Vee](/wiki/Bobby_Vee "Bobby Vee"), playing piano and clapping.[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27) In September 1959, Dylan enrolled at the [University of Minnesota](/wiki/University_of_Minnesota "University of Minnesota").[[28]](#cite_note-28) Living at the Jewish-centric fraternity [Sigma Alpha Mu](/wiki/Sigma_Alpha_Mu "Sigma Alpha Mu") house, Dylan began to perform at the Ten O'Clock Scholar, a coffeehouse near campus, and became involved in the [Dinkytown](/wiki/Dinkytown "Dinkytown") folk music circuit.[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-No_Direction_Home-30) His focus on rock and roll gave way to [American folk music](/wiki/American_folk_music "American folk music"), as he explained in a 1985 interview:

> The thing about rock'n'roll is that for me anyway it wasn't enough.... There were great catch-phrases and driving pulse rhythms ... but the songs weren't serious or didn't reflect life in a realistic way. I knew that when I got into folk music, it was more of a serious type of thing. The songs are filled with more despair, more sadness, more triumph, more faith in the supernatural, much deeper feelings.[[31]](#cite_note-Crowe-1985-31)

During this period, he began to introduce himself as "Bob Dylan".[[32]](#cite_note-32) In his memoir, he wrote that he considered adopting the surname *Dillon* before unexpectedly seeing poems by [Dylan Thomas](/wiki/Dylan_Thomas "Dylan Thomas"), and deciding to spell his name that way.[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[a 1]](#cite_note-34) In a 2004 interview, he said, "You're born, you know, the wrong names, wrong parents. I mean, that happens. You call yourself what you want to call yourself. This is the land of the free."[[34]](#cite_note-60minutes2005-35)

## Career

### 1960–1962: Move to New York and stardom

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Bob_Dylan_1963_promo_photo_by_Don_Hunstein.jpg/250px-Bob_Dylan_1963_promo_photo_by_Don_Hunstein.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bob_Dylan_1963_promo_photo_by_Don_Hunstein.jpg)

Bob Dylan, 1963 promo photo by Don Hunstein for *The Times They Are a-Changin'* in a recording studio

Dylan dropped out of college in May 1960 at the end of his first year. In January 1961, he traveled to New York City to perform and to visit his musical idol [Woody Guthrie](/wiki/Woody_Guthrie "Woody Guthrie")[[35]](#cite_note-36) at [Greystone Park Psychiatric Hospital](/wiki/Greystone_Park_Psychiatric_Hospital "Greystone Park Psychiatric Hospital") in New Jersey.[[36]](#cite_note-37) Guthrie had been a revelation to Dylan and influenced his early performances. "The songs themselves had the infinite sweep of humanity in them," Dylan wrote in *[Chronicles: Volume One](/wiki/Chronicles:_Volume_One "Chronicles: Volume One")*, his 2004 memoir. Guthrie "was the true voice of the American spirit. I said to myself I was going to be Guthrie's greatest disciple."[[37]](#cite_note-38) He wrote that his early songwriting was also shaped by [Robert Johnson](/wiki/Robert_Johnson "Robert Johnson")'s [blues](/wiki/Blues "Blues")[[38]](#cite_note-chronicles281-39) and what he called the "architectural forms" of [Hank Williams](/wiki/Hank_Williams "Hank Williams")'s [country](/wiki/Country_music "Country music") songs.[[17]](#cite_note-chronicles95-17)

From February 1961, Dylan played at clubs around [Greenwich Village](/wiki/Greenwich_Village "Greenwich Village"), befriending and picking up material from folk singers, including [Dave Van Ronk](/wiki/Dave_Van_Ronk "Dave Van Ronk"), [Fred Neil](/wiki/Fred_Neil "Fred Neil"), [Odetta](/wiki/Odetta "Odetta"), the [New Lost City Ramblers](/wiki/New_Lost_City_Ramblers "New Lost City Ramblers") and the Irish musicians [the Clancy Brothers and Tommy Makem](/wiki/The_Clancy_Brothers_and_Tommy_Makem "The Clancy Brothers and Tommy Makem").[[39]](#cite_note-40) In September, the *[New York Times](/wiki/New_York_Times "New York Times")* critic [Robert Shelton](/wiki/Robert_Shelton_(critic) "Robert Shelton (critic)") boosted Dylan's career with an enthusiastic review of his performance at [Gerde's Folk City](/wiki/Gerde%27s_Folk_City "Gerde's Folk City"): "Bob Dylan: A Distinctive Folk-Song Stylist".[[40]](#cite_note-41) That month, Dylan played harmonica on folk singer [Carolyn Hester](/wiki/Carolyn_Hester "Carolyn Hester")'s third album, bringing him to the attention of the album's producer [John Hammond](/wiki/John_Hammond_(record_producer) "John Hammond (record producer)"),[[41]](#cite_note-42) who signed Dylan to [Columbia Records](/wiki/Columbia_Records "Columbia Records").[[42]](#cite_note-43) Dylan's debut album, *[Bob Dylan](/wiki/Bob_Dylan_(album) "Bob Dylan (album)")*, released March 19, 1962,[[43]](#cite_note-44)[[44]](#cite_note-45) consisted of traditional folk, blues and [gospel](/wiki/Gospel_music "Gospel music") material with just two original compositions, "[Talkin' New York](/wiki/Talkin%27_New_York "Talkin' New York")" and "[Song to Woody](/wiki/Song_to_Woody "Song to Woody")". The album sold 5,000 copies in its first year, just breaking even.[[45]](#cite_note-Scaduto110-46)

[![Dylan is seated, singing and playing guitar. Seated to his right is a woman gazing upward and singing with him.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/Joan_Baez_Bob_Dylan.jpg/250px-Joan_Baez_Bob_Dylan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Joan_Baez_Bob_Dylan.jpg)

[Joan Baez](/wiki/Joan_Baez "Joan Baez") and Dylan during the civil rights "[March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom](/wiki/March_on_Washington_for_Jobs_and_Freedom "March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom")", August 28, 1963

On August 9, 1962, Dylan legally changed his name to Robert Dylan in the [St. Louis County Court](/wiki/St._Louis_County_District_Courthouse "St. Louis County District Courthouse"), Hibbing. His father, Abraham Zimmerman, was the witness.[[46]](#cite_note-Legal-47) That month, Dylan signed a management contract with [Albert Grossman](/wiki/Albert_Grossman "Albert Grossman").[[47]](#cite_note-48) Grossman remained Dylan's manager until 1970, and was known for his sometimes confrontational personality and protective loyalty.[[48]](#cite_note-49) Dylan said, "He was kind of like a [Colonel Tom Parker](/wiki/Colonel_Tom_Parker "Colonel Tom Parker") figure ... you could smell him coming."[[30]](#cite_note-No_Direction_Home-30) Tension between Grossman and John Hammond led to Hammond's suggesting that Dylan work with the jazz producer [Tom Wilson](/wiki/Tom_Wilson_(producer) "Tom Wilson (producer)"), who produced several tracks for Dylan's second album without receiving formal credit. Wilson also produced Dylan's next three albums.[[49]](#cite_note-50)[[50]](#cite_note-51)

Dylan made his first visit to the United Kingdom from December 1962 to January 1963.[[51]](#cite_note-Heylin-p35-52) He had been invited by the television director [Philip Saville](/wiki/Philip_Saville "Philip Saville") to appear in the film *[Madhouse on Castle Street](/wiki/Madhouse_on_Castle_Street "Madhouse on Castle Street")*, which Saville was directing for [BBC Television](/wiki/BBC_Television "BBC Television"). At the end of the play, Dylan performed "[Blowin' in the Wind](/wiki/Blowin%27_in_the_Wind "Blowin' in the Wind")", one of its first public performances.[[52]](#cite_note-Flash-back-53) While in London, Dylan performed at several folk clubs, including [the Troubadour](/wiki/The_Troubadour,_London "The Troubadour, London"), [Les Cousins](/wiki/Les_Cousins_(music_club) "Les Cousins (music club)"), and [Bunjies](/wiki/Bunjies "Bunjies").[[51]](#cite_note-Heylin-p35-52)[[53]](#cite_note-54) He also learned material from UK performers, including [Martin Carthy](/wiki/Martin_Carthy "Martin Carthy").[[52]](#cite_note-Flash-back-53)

With the release of his second album, *[The Freewheelin' Bob Dylan](/wiki/The_Freewheelin%27_Bob_Dylan "The Freewheelin' Bob Dylan")*, in May 1963, Dylan began to make his name as a singer-songwriter. Many of the songs were [protest songs](/wiki/Protest_song "Protest song"), inspired by Guthrie and influenced by [Pete Seeger](/wiki/Pete_Seeger "Pete Seeger")'s passion for topical songs.[[54]](#cite_note-55) "[Oxford Town](/wiki/Oxford_Town "Oxford Town")" was an account of [James Meredith](/wiki/James_Meredith "James Meredith")'s ordeal as the first Black student at the [University of Mississippi](/wiki/University_of_Mississippi "University of Mississippi").[[55]](#cite_note-56) The album's first song, "Blowin' in the Wind", partly derived its melody from the traditional [slave song](/wiki/Slave_Songs_of_the_United_States "Slave Songs of the United States") "No More Auction Block",[[56]](#cite_note-57) while its lyrics wondered whether war and injustice would ever end. The song was widely recorded by other artists and became a hit for [Peter, Paul, and Mary](/wiki/Peter,_Paul,_and_Mary "Peter, Paul, and Mary").[[57]](#cite_note-PPM-58) "[A Hard Rain's a-Gonna Fall](/wiki/A_Hard_Rain%27s_a-Gonna_Fall "A Hard Rain's a-Gonna Fall")" was based on the folk ballad "[Lord Randall](/wiki/Lord_Randall "Lord Randall")". With its apocalyptic premonitions, the song gained resonance when the [Cuban Missile Crisis](/wiki/Cuban_Missile_Crisis "Cuban Missile Crisis") occurred a month after Dylan began performing it.[[58]](#cite_note-59)[[59]](#cite_note-60)[[a 2]](#cite_note-61) Both songs marked a new direction in songwriting, blending a [stream-of-consciousness](/wiki/Stream-of-consciousness "Stream-of-consciousness"), [imagist](/wiki/Imagist "Imagist") lyrical attack with traditional folk form.[[60]](#cite_note-62) Dylan's topical songs led to his being viewed as more than just a songwriter. [Janet Maslin](/wiki/Janet_Maslin "Janet Maslin") wrote of *Freewheelin'*:

> These were the songs that established [Dylan] as the voice of his generation —someone who implicitly understood how concerned young Americans felt about [nuclear disarmament](/wiki/Nuclear_disarmament "Nuclear disarmament") and the growing [civil rights movement](/wiki/Civil_rights_movement "Civil rights movement"): his mixture of moral authority and nonconformity was perhaps the most timely of his attributes.[[61]](#cite_note-63)[[a 3]](#cite_note-64)

*Freewheelin'* also included love songs and surreal [talking blues](/wiki/Talking_blues "Talking blues"). Humor was an important part of Dylan's persona,[[62]](#cite_note-65) and the range of material on the album impressed listeners, including [the Beatles](/wiki/The_Beatles "The Beatles"). [George Harrison](/wiki/George_Harrison "George Harrison") said: "We just played it, just wore it out. The content of the song lyrics and just the attitude — it was incredibly original and wonderful."[[63]](#cite_note-66)

The rough edge of Dylan's singing unsettled some, but attracted others. The author [Joyce Carol Oates](/wiki/Joyce_Carol_Oates "Joyce Carol Oates") wrote: "When we first heard this raw, very young, and seemingly untrained voice, frankly nasal, as if sandpaper could sing, the effect was dramatic and electrifying."[[64]](#cite_note-67) Many early Dylan songs reached the public through more palatable versions by other performers, such as [Joan Baez](/wiki/Joan_Baez "Joan Baez"), who became Dylan's advocate and lover.[[65]](#cite_note-68) Baez was influential in bringing Dylan to prominence by recording several of his early songs and inviting him on stage during her concerts.[[66]](#cite_note-69) "[Mixed-Up Confusion](/wiki/Mixed-Up_Confusion "Mixed-Up Confusion")", recorded during the *Freewheelin'* sessions with a backing band, was released as Dylan's first single in December 1962, but swiftly withdrawn. In contrast to the mostly solo acoustic performances on the album, the single showed a willingness to experiment with a [rockabilly](/wiki/Rockabilly "Rockabilly") sound. [Cameron Crowe](/wiki/Cameron_Crowe "Cameron Crowe") described it as "a fascinating look at a folk artist with his mind wandering towards Elvis Presley and [Sun Records](/wiki/Sun_Records "Sun Records")".[[67]](#cite_note-70)

### 1963–1965: Protest music and *Another Side*

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Gnome-mime-audio-openclipart.svg/60px-Gnome-mime-audio-openclipart.svg.png)

["The Times They Are a-Changin'"](/wiki/File:Bob_Dylan_-_The_Times_They_Are_a-Changin%27.ogg "File:Bob Dylan - The Times They Are a-Changin'.ogg")

Dylan said of "The Times They Are a-Changin'": "This was definitely a song with a purpose. I wanted to write a big song, some kind of theme song, with short concise verses that piled up on each other in a hypnotic way. The civil rights movement and the folk music movement were pretty close and allied together at that time."[[31]](#cite_note-Crowe-1985-31)

---

*Problems playing this file? See [media help](/wiki/Help:Media "Help:Media").*

In May 1963, Dylan's political profile rose when he walked out of *[The Ed Sullivan Show](/wiki/The_Ed_Sullivan_Show "The Ed Sullivan Show")*. During rehearsals, Dylan had been told by CBS television's head of program practices that "[Talkin' John Birch Paranoid Blues](/wiki/Talkin%27_John_Birch_Paranoid_Blues "Talkin' John Birch Paranoid Blues")" was potentially [libelous](/wiki/Libelous "Libelous") to the [John Birch Society](/wiki/John_Birch_Society "John Birch Society"). Rather than comply with censorship, Dylan refused to appear.[[68]](#cite_note-71) Dylan and Baez were prominent in the civil rights movement, singing together at the [March on Washington](/wiki/March_on_Washington "March on Washington") on August 28, 1963. Dylan performed "[Only a Pawn in Their Game](/wiki/Only_a_Pawn_in_Their_Game "Only a Pawn in Their Game")" and "[When the Ship Comes In](/wiki/When_the_Ship_Comes_In "When the Ship Comes In")".[[69]](#cite_note-72)

Dylan's third album, *[The Times They Are a-Changin'](/wiki/The_Times_They_Are_a-Changin%27_(Bob_Dylan_album) "The Times They Are a-Changin' (Bob Dylan album)")*, reflected a more politicized Dylan.[[70]](#cite_note-73) The songs often took as their subject matter contemporary stories, with "[Only a Pawn in Their Game](/wiki/Only_a_Pawn_in_Their_Game "Only a Pawn in Their Game")" addressing the murder of civil rights worker [Medgar Evers](/wiki/Medgar_Evers "Medgar Evers"), and the [Brechtian](/wiki/Brechtian "Brechtian") "[The Lonesome Death of Hattie Carroll](/wiki/The_Lonesome_Death_of_Hattie_Carroll "The Lonesome Death of Hattie Carroll")" the death of Black hotel barmaid Hattie Carroll at the hands of young White socialite William Zantzinger.[[71]](#cite_note-74) "[Ballad of Hollis Brown](/wiki/Ballad_of_Hollis_Brown "Ballad of Hollis Brown")" and "[North Country Blues](/wiki/North_Country_Blues "North Country Blues")" addressed despair engendered by the breakdown of farming and mining communities.[[72]](#cite_note-75)

The final track on the album expressed Dylan's angry response to a hostile profile published in *[Newsweek](/wiki/Newsweek "Newsweek")*.[[73]](#cite_note-Svedb-76) As biographer [Clinton Heylin](/wiki/Clinton_Heylin "Clinton Heylin") puts it, the profile wrote about "the way the Bar Mitzvah boy from Hibbing, Minnesota, had reinvented himself as the prince of protest", emphasising his birth name, his attendance at the University of Minnesota, and his close relationship with his parents from whom he claimed to be estranged.[[73]](#cite_note-Svedb-76)[[74]](#cite_note-77) The day after the article appeared, Dylan returned to the studio to record "[Restless Farewell](/wiki/Restless_Farewell "Restless Farewell")" which ends with his vow to "make my stand/ And remain as I am/ And bid farewell and not give a damn".[[75]](#cite_note-78)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/Bob_Dylan_1963_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Bob_Dylan_1963_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bob_Dylan_1963_(cropped).jpg)

Bob Dylan performing in 1963

By the end of 1963, Dylan felt manipulated and constrained by the folk and protest movements.[[76]](#cite_note-79) Accepting the "[Tom Paine](/wiki/Tom_Paine "Tom Paine") Award" from the [Emergency Civil Liberties Committee](/wiki/Emergency_Civil_Liberties_Committee "Emergency Civil Liberties Committee") three weeks after the [assassination of John F. Kennedy](/wiki/Assassination_of_John_F._Kennedy "Assassination of John F. Kennedy"), an intoxicated Dylan questioned the role of the committee, characterized the members as old and balding, and claimed to see something of himself and of every man in Kennedy's assassin, [Lee Harvey Oswald](/wiki/Lee_Harvey_Oswald "Lee Harvey Oswald").[[77]](#cite_note-80)

[Johnny Cash](/wiki/Johnny_Cash "Johnny Cash") supported Dylan in his refusal to conform when he wrote a letter to *[Broadside](/wiki/Broadside_(magazine) "Broadside (magazine)")* magazine in March 1964, expressing admiration for Dylan's writing and ending with the injunction: "Shut up! And let him sing!"[[78]](#cite_note-81) When Cash died in 2003, Dylan remembered: "Johnny wrote the magazine saying to shut up and let me sing, that I knew what I was doing. This was before I had ever met him, and the letter meant the world to me."[[79]](#cite_note-82)[[80]](#cite_note-cashinfluence-83) Cash and Dylan met for the first time at the Newport Folk Festival in July 1964 and became friends.[[81]](#cite_note-84)

*[Another Side of Bob Dylan](/wiki/Another_Side_of_Bob_Dylan "Another Side of Bob Dylan")*, recorded in a single evening on June 9, 1964,[[82]](#cite_note-85) had a lighter mood. The humorous Dylan reemerged on "[I Shall Be Free No. 10](/wiki/I_Shall_Be_Free_No._10 "I Shall Be Free No. 10")" and "Motorpsycho Nightmare". "[Spanish Harlem Incident](/wiki/Spanish_Harlem_Incident "Spanish Harlem Incident")" and "[To Ramona](/wiki/To_Ramona "To Ramona")" are passionate love songs, while "[Black Crow Blues](/wiki/Black_Crow_Blues "Black Crow Blues")" and "[I Don't Believe You (She Acts Like We Never Have Met)](/wiki/I_Don%27t_Believe_You_(She_Acts_Like_We_Never_Have_Met) "I Don't Believe You (She Acts Like We Never Have Met)")" suggest the rock and roll soon to dominate Dylan's music. "[It Ain't Me Babe](/wiki/It_Ain%27t_Me_Babe "It Ain't Me Babe")", on the surface a song about spurned love, has been described as a rejection of the role of political spokesman thrust upon him.[[83]](#cite_note-86) His new direction was signaled by two lengthy songs: the impressionistic "[Chimes of Freedom](/wiki/Chimes_of_Freedom_(song) "Chimes of Freedom (song)")", which sets [social commentary](/wiki/Social_commentary "Social commentary") against a metaphorical landscape in a style characterized by [Allen Ginsberg](/wiki/Allen_Ginsberg "Allen Ginsberg") as "chains of flashing images,"[[a 4]](#cite_note-87) and "[My Back Pages](/wiki/My_Back_Pages "My Back Pages")", which attacks the simplistic seriousness of his own earlier topical songs and seems to predict the backlash he was about to encounter from his former champions.[[84]](#cite_note-88)

In late 1964 and early 1965, Dylan moved from folk songwriter to [folk-rock](/wiki/Folk-rock "Folk-rock") pop-music star. His jeans and work shirts were replaced by a [Carnaby Street](/wiki/Carnaby_Street "Carnaby Street") wardrobe, sunglasses day and night, and pointed "[Beatle boots](/wiki/Beatle_boot "Beatle boot")". A London reporter noted "Hair that would set the teeth of a comb on edge. A loud shirt that would dim the neon lights of [Leicester Square](/wiki/Leicester_Square "Leicester Square"). He looks like an undernourished [cockatoo](/wiki/Cockatoo "Cockatoo")."[[85]](#cite_note-89) Dylan began to spar with interviewers. While on [Les Crane](/wiki/Les_Crane "Les Crane")'s television show, asked about a movie he planned, he told Crane it would be a "cowboy horror movie". Asked if he played the cowboy, Dylan replied, "No, I play my mother."[[86]](#cite_note-90)

### 1965–1969: Going electric and motorcycle accident

Main articles: [Electric Dylan controversy](/wiki/Electric_Dylan_controversy "Electric Dylan controversy") and [Folk rock](/wiki/Folk_rock "Folk rock")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Bob_Dylan_%281965%29.jpg/250px-Bob_Dylan_%281965%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bob_Dylan_(1965).jpg)

Dylan in 1965

Dylan's late March 1965 album, *[Bringing It All Back Home](/wiki/Bringing_It_All_Back_Home "Bringing It All Back Home")*, was another leap,[[87]](#cite_note-91) featuring his first recordings with electric instruments, under producer Tom Wilson's guidance.[[88]](#cite_note-92) The first single, "[Subterranean Homesick Blues](/wiki/Subterranean_Homesick_Blues "Subterranean Homesick Blues")", owed much to [Chuck Berry](/wiki/Chuck_Berry "Chuck Berry")'s "[Too Much Monkey Business](/wiki/Too_Much_Monkey_Business "Too Much Monkey Business")",[[89]](#cite_note-93) its free-association lyrics described as harking back to the energy of [beat poetry](/wiki/Beat_poetry "Beat poetry") and as a forerunner of [rap](/wiki/Rap "Rap") and [hip-hop](/wiki/Hip-hop "Hip-hop").[[90]](#cite_note-94) The album's second side contained four long songs on which Dylan accompanied himself on acoustic guitar and harmonica.[[91]](#cite_note-Sounes-168-95) "[Mr. Tambourine Man](/wiki/Mr._Tambourine_Man "Mr. Tambourine Man")" became one of his best-known songs when [The Byrds](/wiki/The_Byrds "The Byrds") recorded an electric version that reached number one in the US and UK.[[92]](#cite_note-96)[[93]](#cite_note-97) The album's final two songs, "[It's All Over Now, Baby Blue](/wiki/It%27s_All_Over_Now,_Baby_Blue "It's All Over Now, Baby Blue")" and "[It's Alright, Ma (I'm Only Bleeding)](/wiki/It%27s_Alright,_Ma_(I%27m_Only_Bleeding) "It's Alright, Ma (I'm Only Bleeding)")", are among Dylan's most important compositions.[[91]](#cite_note-Sounes-168-95)[[94]](#cite_note-98)

From April 30 to May 10, 1965, Dylan played an [eight-concert tour in England](/wiki/Bob_Dylan_England_Tour_1965 "Bob Dylan England Tour 1965"). The tour would be memorialized in *[Dont Look Back](/wiki/Dont_Look_Back "Dont Look Back"),* [D. A. Pennebaker](/wiki/D._A._Pennebaker "D. A. Pennebaker")'s 1967 [cinéma vérité](/wiki/Cin%C3%A9ma_v%C3%A9rit%C3%A9 "Cinéma vérité") presentation.[[95]](#cite_note-99) The movie opens with an early [music video](/wiki/Music_video "Music video") for "Subterranean Homesick Blues" in which Dylan, instead of [lipsynching](/wiki/Lipsynching "Lipsynching") the lyrics, dropped cue cards containing key words to the ground. Pennebaker said the sequence was Dylan's idea; it has been imitated in music videos and advertisements.[[96]](#cite_note-100)

On July 25, 1965, headlining the [Newport Folk Festival](/wiki/Newport_Folk_Festival "Newport Folk Festival"), Dylan performed his first electric set since high school with a [pickup group](/wiki/Pickup_group "Pickup group") featuring [Mike Bloomfield](/wiki/Mike_Bloomfield "Mike Bloomfield") on guitar and [Al Kooper](/wiki/Al_Kooper "Al Kooper") on organ.[[97]](#cite_note-101) Dylan had appeared at Newport in 1963 and 1964, but in 1965 he was met with cheering and booing and left the stage after three songs. One version has it that the boos were from folk fans whom Dylan had alienated by appearing, unexpectedly, with an electric guitar. [Murray Lerner](/wiki/Murray_Lerner "Murray Lerner"), who filmed the performance, said: "I absolutely think that they were booing Dylan going electric."[[98]](#cite_note-102) An alternative account claims audience members were upset by poor sound and a short set.[[99]](#cite_note-103)[[100]](#cite_note-104)

Dylan's performance provoked a hostile response from the folk music establishment.[[101]](#cite_note-105)[[102]](#cite_note-106) In the September issue of *[Sing Out!](/wiki/Sing_Out! "Sing Out!")*, [Ewan MacColl](/wiki/Ewan_MacColl "Ewan MacColl") wrote: "Our traditional songs and ballads are the creations of extraordinarily talented artists working inside disciplines formulated over time.... 'But what of Bobby Dylan?' scream the outraged teenagers.... Only a completely non-critical audience, nourished on the watery pap of pop music, could have fallen for such tenth-rate drivel".[[103]](#cite_note-107) On July 29, four days after Newport, Dylan was back in the studio in New York, recording "[Positively 4th Street](/wiki/Positively_4th_Street "Positively 4th Street")". The lyrics contained images of vengeance and paranoia,[[104]](#cite_note-108) and have been interpreted as Dylan's put-down of former friends from the folk community he had known in clubs along [West 4th Street](/wiki/4th_Street_(Manhattan) "4th Street (Manhattan)").[[105]](#cite_note-109)

***Highway 61 Revisited* and *Blonde on Blonde***

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Gnome-mime-audio-openclipart.svg/60px-Gnome-mime-audio-openclipart.svg.png)

["Like a Rolling Stone"](/wiki/File:Bob_Dylan_-_Like_a_Rolling_Stone.ogg "File:Bob Dylan - Like a Rolling Stone.ogg")

Dylan's 1965 hit single, which appeared on the album *[Highway 61 Revisited](/wiki/Highway_61_Revisited "Highway 61 Revisited")*. In 2004, it was chosen as the greatest song of all time by *[Rolling Stone](/wiki/Rolling_Stone "Rolling Stone")* magazine.[[106]](#cite_note-RS500-110)

---

*Problems playing this file? See [media help](/wiki/Help:Media "Help:Media").*

In July 1965, Dylan's six-minute single "[Like a Rolling Stone](/wiki/Like_a_Rolling_Stone "Like a Rolling Stone")" peaked at number two in the US chart. In 2004 and in 2011, *[Rolling Stone](/wiki/Rolling_Stone "Rolling Stone")* listed it as number one on "[The 500 Greatest Songs of All Time](/wiki/The_500_Greatest_Songs_of_All_Time "The 500 Greatest Songs of All Time")".[[107]](#cite_note-LARS-111)[[108]](#cite_note-112)[[106]](#cite_note-RS500-110) [Bruce Springsteen](/wiki/Bruce_Springsteen "Bruce Springsteen") recalled first hearing the song: "that snare shot sounded like somebody'd kicked open the door to your mind."[[109]](#cite_note-Springsteen-113) The song opened Dylan's next album, *[Highway 61 Revisited](/wiki/Highway_61_Revisited "Highway 61 Revisited")*, named after the road that led from [Fort William, Ontario](/wiki/Fort_William,_Ontario "Fort William, Ontario") through Dylan's Minnesota to the musical hotbed of [New Orleans](/wiki/New_Orleans "New Orleans").[[110]](#cite_note-114) The songs were in the same vein as the hit single, flavored by Mike Bloomfield's blues guitar and Al Kooper's organ riffs. "[Desolation Row](/wiki/Desolation_Row "Desolation Row")", backed by acoustic guitar and understated bass,[[111]](#cite_note-115) offers the sole exception, with Dylan alluding to figures in Western culture in a song described by Andy Gill as "an 11-minute epic of entropy, which takes the form of a [Fellini-esque](/wiki/Federico_Fellini "Federico Fellini") parade of grotesques and oddities featuring a huge cast of celebrated characters".[[112]](#cite_note-116) Poet [Philip Larkin](/wiki/Philip_Larkin "Philip Larkin"), who also reviewed jazz for *[The Daily Telegraph](/wiki/The_Daily_Telegraph "The Daily Telegraph")*, wrote "I'm afraid I poached Bob Dylan's *Highway 61 Revisited* (CBS) out of curiosity and found myself well rewarded. Dylan's cawing, derisive voice is probably well suited to his material."[[113]](#cite_note-117)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/Dont_Look_Back_-_Bob_Dylan_%281967_film_poster%29.jpg/250px-Dont_Look_Back_-_Bob_Dylan_%281967_film_poster%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dont_Look_Back_-_Bob_Dylan_(1967_film_poster).jpg)

The [cinéma vérité](/wiki/Cin%C3%A9ma_v%C3%A9rit%C3%A9 "Cinéma vérité") documentary *[Dont Look Back](/wiki/Dont_Look_Back "Dont Look Back")* (1967) follows Dylan on his [1965 tour of England](/wiki/Bob_Dylan_England_Tour_1965 "Bob Dylan England Tour 1965"). An early music video for "[Subterranean Homesick Blues](/wiki/Subterranean_Homesick_Blues "Subterranean Homesick Blues")" was used as the film's opening segment.

From September 24, 1965, in Austin, Texas, Dylan toured the US and Canada for six months, backed by the five musicians from the Hawks who became known as [the Band](/wiki/The_Band "The Band").[[114]](#cite_note-118) While Dylan and the Hawks met increasingly receptive audiences, their studio efforts floundered. Producer [Bob Johnston](/wiki/Bob_Johnston "Bob Johnston") persuaded Dylan to record in [Nashville](/wiki/Nashville "Nashville") in February 1966, and surrounded him with top-notch session men. At Dylan's insistence, Robertson and Kooper came from New York City to play on the sessions.[[115]](#cite_note-119) The Nashville sessions produced the double album *[Blonde on Blonde](/wiki/Blonde_on_Blonde "Blonde on Blonde")* (1966), featuring what Dylan called "that thin wild mercury sound".[[116]](#cite_note-120) Kooper described it as "taking two cultures and smashing them together with a huge explosion": the musical worlds of Nashville and of the "quintessential New York hipster" Bob Dylan.[[117]](#cite_note-121)

On November 22, 1965, Dylan quietly married 25-year-old former model [Sara Lownds](/wiki/Sara_Lownds "Sara Lownds").[[118]](#cite_note-Sounes-p193-122) Some of Dylan's friends, including Ramblin' Jack Elliott, say that, immediately after the event, Dylan denied he was married.[[118]](#cite_note-Sounes-p193-122) Writer [Nora Ephron](/wiki/Nora_Ephron "Nora Ephron") made the news public in the *[New York Post](/wiki/New_York_Post "New York Post")* in February 1966 with the headline "Hush! Bob Dylan is wed".[[119]](#cite_note-123)

Dylan toured Australia and Europe in April and May 1966. Each show was split in two. Dylan performed solo during the first half, accompanying himself on [acoustic guitar](/wiki/Steel-string_guitar "Steel-string guitar") and harmonica. In the second, backed by the Hawks, he played electrically amplified music. This contrast provoked many fans, who jeered and [slow clapped](/wiki/Slow_clap "Slow clap").[[120]](#cite_note-124) The tour culminated in a raucous confrontation between Dylan and his audience at the Manchester [Free Trade Hall](/wiki/Free_Trade_Hall "Free Trade Hall") in England on May 17, 1966.[[121]](#cite_note-125) A recording of this concert was released in 1998: *[The Bootleg Series Vol. 4: Bob Dylan Live 1966](/wiki/The_Bootleg_Series_Vol._4:_Bob_Dylan_Live_1966,_The_%22Royal_Albert_Hall%22_Concert "The Bootleg Series Vol. 4: Bob Dylan Live 1966, The \"Royal Albert Hall\" Concert")*. At the climax of the evening, a member of the audience, angered by Dylan's electric backing, shouted: "[Judas](/wiki/Judas "Judas")!" to which Dylan responded, "I don't believe you ... You're a liar!" Dylan turned to his band and said, "Play it fucking loud!"[[122]](#cite_note-126)

Dylan was described as exhausted and behaving as if "he was on some kind of death trip" during his 1966 tour.[[123]](#cite_note-127) D. A. Pennebaker, the filmmaker accompanying the tour, described Dylan as "taking a lot of amphetamine and who-knows-what-else".[[124]](#cite_note-128) In a 1969 interview with [Jann Wenner](/wiki/Jann_Wenner "Jann Wenner"), Dylan said, "I was on the road for almost five years. It wore me down. I was on drugs, a lot of things ... just to keep going, you know?"[[125]](#cite_note-129)

On July 29, 1966, Dylan crashed his motorcycle, a [Triumph Tiger 100](/wiki/Triumph_Tiger_100 "Triumph Tiger 100"), near his home in [Woodstock, New York](/wiki/Woodstock,_New_York "Woodstock, New York"). Dylan said he broke several [vertebrae](/wiki/Vertebrae "Vertebrae") in his neck.[[126]](#cite_note-Sounes-217-130) The circumstances of the accident are unclear since no ambulance was called to the scene and Dylan was not hospitalized.[[126]](#cite_note-Sounes-217-130)[[127]](#cite_note-crash-131) Dylan's biographers have written that the crash offered him the chance to escape the pressures around him.[[126]](#cite_note-Sounes-217-130)[[128]](#cite_note-shades268-132) Dylan concurred: "I had been in a motorcycle accident and I'd been hurt, but I recovered. Truth was that I wanted to get out of the rat race."[[129]](#cite_note-133) He made very few public appearances, and did not tour again for almost eight years.[[127]](#cite_note-crash-131)[[130]](#cite_note-134)

Once Dylan was well enough to resume creative work, he began to edit D. A. Pennebaker's film of his 1966 tour. A rough cut was shown to ABC Television, but they rejected it as incomprehensible to mainstream audiences.[[131]](#cite_note-135) The film, titled *[Eat the Document](/wiki/Eat_the_Document "Eat the Document")* on [bootleg](/wiki/Bootleg_recording "Bootleg recording") copies, has since been screened at a few film festivals.[[132]](#cite_note-136) Secluded from public gaze, Dylan recorded [over 100 songs](/wiki/List_of_Basement_Tapes_songs "List of Basement Tapes songs") during 1967 at his Woodstock home and in the basement of the Hawks' nearby house, "[Big Pink](/wiki/Big_Pink "Big Pink")".[[133]](#cite_note-137) These songs were initially offered as demos for other artists to record and were hits for [Julie Driscoll](/wiki/Julie_Driscoll "Julie Driscoll"), the Byrds, and Manfred Mann. The public heard these recordings when *[Great White Wonder](/wiki/Great_White_Wonder "Great White Wonder")*, the first "[bootleg recording](/wiki/Bootleg_recording "Bootleg recording")", appeared in West Coast shops in July 1969, containing Dylan material recorded in Minneapolis in 1961 and seven Basement Tapes songs. This record gave birth to a minor industry in the illicit release of recordings by Dylan and other major rock artists.[[134]](#cite_note-138) Columbia released a Basement selection in 1975 as *[The Basement Tapes](/wiki/The_Basement_Tapes "The Basement Tapes")*.

In late 1967, Dylan returned to studio recording in Nashville,[[135]](#cite_note-BjornerJWH-139) accompanied by [Charlie McCoy](/wiki/Charlie_McCoy "Charlie McCoy") on bass,[[135]](#cite_note-BjornerJWH-139) [Kenny Buttrey](/wiki/Kenny_Buttrey "Kenny Buttrey") on drums[[135]](#cite_note-BjornerJWH-139) and [Pete Drake](/wiki/Pete_Drake "Pete Drake") on steel guitar.[[135]](#cite_note-BjornerJWH-139) The result was *[John Wesley Harding](/wiki/John_Wesley_Harding "John Wesley Harding")*, a record of short songs thematically drawing on the [American West](/wiki/American_West "American West") and [the Bible](/wiki/The_Bible "The Bible"). The sparse structure and instrumentation, with lyrics that took the [Judeo-Christian](/wiki/Judeo-Christian "Judeo-Christian") tradition seriously, was a departure from Dylan's previous work.[[136]](#cite_note-140) It included "[All Along the Watchtower](/wiki/All_Along_the_Watchtower "All Along the Watchtower")", famously covered by [Jimi Hendrix](/wiki/Jimi_Hendrix "Jimi Hendrix").[[31]](#cite_note-Crowe-1985-31)[[a 5]](#cite_note-141) [Woody Guthrie](/wiki/Woody_Guthrie "Woody Guthrie") died in October 1967, and Dylan made his first live appearance in twenty months at a memorial concert held at [Carnegie Hall](/wiki/Carnegie_Hall "Carnegie Hall") on January 20, 1968, where he was backed by the Band.[[137]](#cite_note-142)

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Gnome-mime-audio-openclipart.svg/60px-Gnome-mime-audio-openclipart.svg.png)

["Lay Lady Lay"](/wiki/File:Lay_Lady_Lay.ogg "File:Lay Lady Lay.ogg")

"[Lay Lady Lay](/wiki/Lay_Lady_Lay "Lay Lady Lay")", on the country album *[Nashville Skyline](/wiki/Nashville_Skyline "Nashville Skyline")*, has been one of Dylan's biggest hits, reaching No. 7 in the US.[[138]](#cite_note-143)

---

*Problems playing this file? See [media help](/wiki/Help:Media "Help:Media").*

*[Nashville Skyline](/wiki/Nashville_Skyline "Nashville Skyline")* (1969), featured Nashville musicians, a mellow-voiced Dylan, a duet with [Johnny Cash](/wiki/Johnny_Cash "Johnny Cash") and the single "[Lay Lady Lay](/wiki/Lay_Lady_Lay "Lay Lady Lay")".[[139]](#cite_note-144) *[Variety](/wiki/Variety_(magazine) "Variety (magazine)")* wrote, "Dylan is definitely doing something that can be called singing. Somehow he has managed to add an octave to his range."[[140]](#cite_note-145) During one recording session, Dylan and Cash recorded a series of duets, but only their version of "[Girl from the North Country](/wiki/Girl_from_the_North_Country "Girl from the North Country")" appeared on the album.[[141]](#cite_note-cashdylan-146)[[142]](#cite_note-mornings-147) The album influenced the nascent genre of [country rock](/wiki/Country_rock "Country rock").[[4]](#cite_note-EncBr-4) Dylan traveled to England to top the bill at the [Isle of Wight Festival](/wiki/Isle_of_Wight_Festival_1969 "Isle of Wight Festival 1969") on August 31, 1969, after rejecting overtures to appear at the [Woodstock Festival](/wiki/Woodstock_Festival "Woodstock Festival") closer to home.[[143]](#cite_note-148)

### 1970–1979: Return to touring and Christian music

In the early 1970s, critics charged that Dylan's output was varied and unpredictable. [Greil Marcus](/wiki/Greil_Marcus "Greil Marcus") asked "What is this shit?" upon first hearing *[Self Portrait](/wiki/Self_Portrait_(Bob_Dylan_album) "Self Portrait (Bob Dylan album)")*, released in June 1970.[[144]](#cite_note-149)[[145]](#cite_note-150) It was a double LP including few original songs and was poorly received.[[146]](#cite_note-151) In October 1970, Dylan released *[New Morning](/wiki/New_Morning "New Morning")*, considered a return to form.[[147]](#cite_note-152) The title track was from Dylan's ill-fated collaboration with MacLeish,[[148]](#cite_note-MacL-153) and "[Day of the Locusts](/wiki/Day_of_the_Locusts "Day of the Locusts")" was his account of receiving an honorary degree from [Princeton University](/wiki/Princeton_University "Princeton University") on June 9, 1970.[[149]](#cite_note-154) In November 1968, Dylan co-wrote "[I'd Have You Anytime](/wiki/I%27d_Have_You_Anytime "I'd Have You Anytime")" with George Harrison;[[150]](#cite_note-155) Harrison recorded that song and Dylan's "[If Not for You](/wiki/If_Not_for_You "If Not for You")" for his album *[All Things Must Pass](/wiki/All_Things_Must_Pass "All Things Must Pass")*. [Olivia Newton-John](/wiki/Olivia_Newton-John "Olivia Newton-John") covered "If Not For You" on her [debut album](/wiki/If_Not_for_You_(album) "If Not for You (album)") and "[The Man in Me](/wiki/The_Man_in_Me "The Man in Me")" was prominently featured in the film *[The Big Lebowski](/wiki/The_Big_Lebowski "The Big Lebowski")* (1998).

*[Tarantula](/wiki/Tarantula_(poetry_collection) "Tarantula (poetry collection)")*, a freeform book of prose-poetry, had been written by Dylan during a creative burst in 1964–65.[[151]](#cite_note-156) Dylan shelved his book for several years, apparently uncertain of its status,[[152]](#cite_note-157) until he suddenly informed [Macmillan](/wiki/Macmillan_Inc. "Macmillan Inc.") at the end of 1970 that the time had come to publish it.[[153]](#cite_note-158) The book attracted negative reviews but later critics have suggested its affinities with *[Finnegans Wake](/wiki/Finnegans_Wake "Finnegans Wake")* and *[A Season in Hell](/wiki/A_Season_in_Hell "A Season in Hell")*.[[154]](#cite_note-159)

Between March 16 and 19, 1971, Dylan recorded with [Leon Russell](/wiki/Leon_Russell "Leon Russell") at [Blue Rock](/wiki/Blue_Rock_Studio "Blue Rock Studio"), a small studio in Greenwich Village. These sessions resulted in "[Watching the River Flow](/wiki/Watching_the_River_Flow "Watching the River Flow")" and a new recording of "[When I Paint My Masterpiece](/wiki/When_I_Paint_My_Masterpiece "When I Paint My Masterpiece")".[[155]](#cite_note-160) On November 4, 1971, Dylan recorded "[George Jackson](/wiki/George_Jackson_(song) "George Jackson (song)")", which he released a week later. For many, the single was a surprising return to protest material, mourning the killing of [Black Panther](/wiki/Black_Panther_Party "Black Panther Party") [George Jackson](/wiki/George_Jackson_(Black_Panther) "George Jackson (Black Panther)") in [San Quentin State Prison](/wiki/San_Quentin_State_Prison "San Quentin State Prison").[[156]](#cite_note-161) Dylan's surprise appearance at Harrison's [Concert for Bangladesh](/wiki/Concert_for_Bangladesh "Concert for Bangladesh") on August 1, 1971, attracted media coverage as his live appearances had become rare.[[157]](#cite_note-162)

In 1972, Dylan joined [Sam Peckinpah](/wiki/Sam_Peckinpah "Sam Peckinpah")'s film *[Pat Garrett and Billy the Kid](/wiki/Pat_Garrett_and_Billy_the_Kid "Pat Garrett and Billy the Kid")*, providing [the soundtrack](/wiki/Pat_Garrett_and_Billy_the_Kid_(album) "Pat Garrett and Billy the Kid (album)") and playing "Alias", a member of Billy's gang.[[158]](#cite_note-163) Despite the film's failure at the box office, "[Knockin' on Heaven's Door](/wiki/Knockin%27_on_Heaven%27s_Door "Knockin' on Heaven's Door")" became one of Dylan's most covered songs.[[159]](#cite_note-164)[[160]](#cite_note-165) That same year, Dylan protested the move to deport [John Lennon](/wiki/John_Lennon "John Lennon") and [Yoko Ono](/wiki/Yoko_Ono "Yoko Ono"), who had been convicted for [marijuana](/wiki/Marijuana "Marijuana") possession, by sending a letter to the US [Immigration Service](/wiki/Immigration_and_Naturalization_Service "Immigration and Naturalization Service") which read in part: "Hurray for John & Yoko. Let them stay and live here and breathe. The country's got plenty of room and space. Let John and Yoko stay!"[[161]](#cite_note-166)

[![Dylan together with three musicians from The Band onstage. Dylan is third from left, wearing a black jacket and pants. He is singing and playing an electric guitar.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Bob_Dylan_and_The_Band_-_1974.jpg/250px-Bob_Dylan_and_The_Band_-_1974.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bob_Dylan_and_The_Band_-_1974.jpg)

Bob Dylan and [the Band](/wiki/The_Band "The Band") commenced their 1974 tour in Chicago on January 3, 1974.[[162]](#cite_note-167)

Dylan began 1973 by signing with a new label, [David Geffen](/wiki/David_Geffen "David Geffen")'s [Asylum Records](/wiki/Asylum_Records "Asylum Records"), when his contract with Columbia Records expired.[[163]](#cite_note-168) His next album, *[Planet Waves](/wiki/Planet_Waves "Planet Waves")*, was recorded in the fall of 1973, using the Band as his backing group as they rehearsed for a major tour.[[164]](#cite_note-169) The album included two versions of ["Forever Young"](/wiki/Forever_Young_(Bob_Dylan_song) "Forever Young (Bob Dylan song)"), which became one of his most popular songs.[[165]](#cite_note-Sounes-p273-170) As one critic described it, the song projected "something hymnal and heartfelt that spoke of the father in Dylan",[[166]](#cite_note-171) and Dylan said "I wrote it thinking about one of my boys and not wanting to be too sentimental".[[31]](#cite_note-Crowe-1985-31) Columbia Records simultaneously released *[Dylan](/wiki/Dylan_(1973_album) "Dylan (1973 album)")*, a collection of studio outtakes, widely interpreted as a churlish response to Dylan's signing with a rival record label.[[167]](#cite_note-172)

In January 1974, Dylan, backed by the Band, embarked on a [North American tour](/wiki/Bob_Dylan_and_The_Band_1974_Tour "Bob Dylan and The Band 1974 Tour") of 40 concerts—his first tour for seven years. A live double album, *[Before the Flood](/wiki/Before_the_Flood_(album) "Before the Flood (album)")*, was released on Asylum Records. Soon, according to [Clive Davis](/wiki/Clive_Davis "Clive Davis"), Columbia Records sent word they "will spare nothing to bring Dylan back into the fold".[[168]](#cite_note-Shelton-378-173) Dylan had second thoughts about Asylum, unhappy that Geffen had sold only 600,000 copies of *Planet Waves* despite millions of unfulfilled ticket requests for the 1974 tour;[[169]](#cite_note-174) he returned to Columbia Records, which reissued his two Asylum albums.[[170]](#cite_note-175)

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Gnome-mime-audio-openclipart.svg/60px-Gnome-mime-audio-openclipart.svg.png)

["Tangled Up in Blue"](/wiki/File:Tangled_Up_In_Blue.ogg "File:Tangled Up In Blue.ogg")

Dylan said of the opening song from *[Blood on the Tracks](/wiki/Blood_on_the_Tracks "Blood on the Tracks")*: "I was trying to deal with the concept of time, and the way the characters change from the first person to the third person, and you're never sure if the first person is talking or the third person. But as you look at the whole thing it really doesn't matter."[[31]](#cite_note-Crowe-1985-31)

---

*Problems playing this file? See [media help](/wiki/Help:Media "Help:Media").*

After the tour, Dylan and his wife became estranged. He filled three small notebooks with songs about relationships and ruptures, and recorded the album *[Blood on the Tracks](/wiki/Blood_on_the_Tracks "Blood on the Tracks")* in September 1974.[[171]](#cite_note-176)[[172]](#cite_note-177) Dylan delayed the album's release and re-recorded half the songs at [Sound 80](/wiki/Sound_80 "Sound 80") Studios in Minneapolis with production assistance from his brother, David Zimmerman.[[173]](#cite_note-178) Released in early 1975, *Blood on the Tracks* received mixed reviews. In *[NME](/wiki/NME "NME")*, [Nick Kent](/wiki/Nick_Kent "Nick Kent") described the "accompaniments" as "often so trashy they sound like mere practice takes".[[174]](#cite_note-Heylin_383-179) In *Rolling Stone*, [Jon Landau](/wiki/Jon_Landau "Jon Landau") wrote that "the record has been made with typical shoddiness".[[174]](#cite_note-Heylin_383-179) Over the years critics came to see it as one of Dylan's masterpieces. In *[Salon](/wiki/Salon_(website) "Salon (website)")*, journalist Bill Wyman wrote:

> *Blood on the Tracks* is his only flawless album and his best produced; the songs, each of them, are constructed in disciplined fashion. It is his kindest album and most dismayed, and seems in hindsight to have achieved a sublime balance between the logorrhea-plagued excesses of his mid-1960s output and the self-consciously simple compositions of his post-accident years.[[175]](#cite_note-180)

[![Dylan, wearing a hat and leather coat, plays guitar and sings, seated. Crouched next to him is a bearded man, listening to him with head bent.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/Allen_Ginsberg_and_Bob_Dylan_by_Elsa_Dorfman.jpg/250px-Allen_Ginsberg_and_Bob_Dylan_by_Elsa_Dorfman.jpg)](/wiki/File:Allen_Ginsberg_and_Bob_Dylan_by_Elsa_Dorfman.jpg)

Bob Dylan with [Allen Ginsberg](/wiki/Allen_Ginsberg "Allen Ginsberg") on the [Rolling Thunder Revue](/wiki/Rolling_Thunder_Revue "Rolling Thunder Revue") in 1975

In the middle of 1975, Dylan championed boxer [Rubin "Hurricane" Carter](/wiki/Rubin_%22Hurricane%22_Carter "Rubin \"Hurricane\" Carter"), imprisoned for triple murder, with his ballad "[Hurricane](/wiki/Hurricane_(Bob_Dylan_song) "Hurricane (Bob Dylan song)")" making the case for Carter's innocence. Despite its length—over eight minutes—the song was released as a single, peaking at 33 on the US [Billboard chart](/wiki/Billboard_Hot_100 "Billboard Hot 100"), and performed at every 1975 date of Dylan's next tour, the [Rolling Thunder Revue](/wiki/Rolling_Thunder_Revue "Rolling Thunder Revue").[[a 6]](#cite_note-181)[[176]](#cite_note-182) Running through late 1975 and again through early 1976, the tour featured about one hundred performers and supporters from the Greenwich Village folk scene, among them Ramblin' Jack Elliott, [T-Bone Burnett](/wiki/T-Bone_Burnett "T-Bone Burnett"), [Joni Mitchell](/wiki/Joni_Mitchell "Joni Mitchell"),[[177]](#cite_note-Kokay-183)[[178]](#cite_note-Sloman-184) [David Mansfield](/wiki/David_Mansfield "David Mansfield"), [Roger McGuinn](/wiki/Roger_McGuinn "Roger McGuinn"), [Mick Ronson](/wiki/Mick_Ronson "Mick Ronson"), [Ronee Blakely](/wiki/Ronee_Blakely "Ronee Blakely"), Joan Baez and [Scarlet Rivera](/wiki/Scarlet_Rivera "Scarlet Rivera"), whom Dylan discovered walking down the street, her violin case on her back.[[179]](#cite_note-185) The tour encompassed the January 1976 release of the album *[Desire](/wiki/Desire_(Bob_Dylan_album) "Desire (Bob Dylan album)")*. Many of *Desire'*s songs featuring a [travelogue](/wiki/Travel_literature "Travel literature")-like narrative style, influenced by Dylan's new collaborator, playwright [Jacques Levy](/wiki/Jacques_Levy "Jacques Levy").[[180]](#cite_note-186)[[181]](#cite_note-187) The 1976 half of the tour was documented by a TV concert special, *Hard Rain*, and the LP *[Hard Rain](/wiki/Hard_Rain_(Bob_Dylan_album) "Hard Rain (Bob Dylan album)")*.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Bob_Dylan_1978.jpg/250px-Bob_Dylan_1978.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bob_Dylan_1978.jpg)

Dylan performing in the [De Kuip](/wiki/De_Kuip "De Kuip") Stadium, Rotterdam, June 23, 1978

The 1975 tour with the Revue provided the backdrop to Dylan's film *[Renaldo and Clara](/wiki/Renaldo_and_Clara "Renaldo and Clara")*, a sprawling narrative mixed with concert footage and reminiscences. Actor and playwright [Sam Shepard](/wiki/Sam_Shepard "Sam Shepard") accompanied the Revue and was to serve as screenwriter, but much of the film was improvised. Released in 1978, it received negative, sometimes scathing, reviews.[[182]](#cite_note-188)[[183]](#cite_note-189) Later in the year, a two-hour edit, dominated by the concert performances, was more widely released.[[184]](#cite_note-190) In November 1976, Dylan appeared at the Band's farewell concert with [Eric Clapton](/wiki/Eric_Clapton "Eric Clapton"), [Muddy Waters](/wiki/Muddy_Waters "Muddy Waters"), [Van Morrison](/wiki/Van_Morrison "Van Morrison"), [Neil Young](/wiki/Neil_Young "Neil Young") and Joni Mitchell. [Martin Scorsese](/wiki/Martin_Scorsese "Martin Scorsese")'s 1978 film of the concert, *[The Last Waltz](/wiki/The_Last_Waltz "The Last Waltz")*, included most of Dylan's set.[[185]](#cite_note-191)
In 1978, Dylan embarked on a [year-long world tour](/wiki/1978_Tour "1978 Tour"), performing 114 shows in Japan, the Far East, Europe and North America, to a total audience of two million. Dylan assembled an eight-piece band and three backing singers. Concerts in Tokyo in February and March were released as the live double album *[Bob Dylan at Budokan](/wiki/Bob_Dylan_at_Budokan "Bob Dylan at Budokan")*.[[186]](#cite_note-Sounes314-192) Reviews were mixed. [Robert Christgau](/wiki/Robert_Christgau "Robert Christgau") awarded the album a C+ rating,[[187]](#cite_note-193) while Janet Maslin defended it: "These latest live versions of his old songs have the effect of liberating Bob Dylan from the originals".[[188]](#cite_note-194) When Dylan brought the tour to the US in September 1978, the press described the look and sound as a "Las Vegas Tour".[[189]](#cite_note-195) The 1978 tour grossed more than $20 million, and Dylan told the *Los Angeles Times* that he had debts because "I had a couple of bad years. I put a lot of money into the movie, built a big house  ... and it costs a lot to get divorced in California."[[186]](#cite_note-Sounes314-192) In April and May 1978, Dylan took the same band and vocalists into Rundown Studios in [Santa Monica](/wiki/Santa_Monica "Santa Monica"), California, to record an album of new material, *[Street-Legal](/wiki/Street-Legal_(album) "Street-Legal (album)")*.[[190]](#cite_note-196) It was described by [Michael Gray](/wiki/Michael_Gray_(author) "Michael Gray (author)") as "after *Blood On The Tracks*, arguably Dylan's best record of the 1970s: a crucial album documenting a crucial period in Dylan's own life".[[191]](#cite_note-197) However, it had poor sound and mixing (attributed to Dylan's studio practices), muddying the instrumental detail until a remastered CD release in 1999 restored some of the songs' strengths.[[192]](#cite_note-198)[[193]](#cite_note-199)

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Gnome-mime-audio-openclipart.svg/60px-Gnome-mime-audio-openclipart.svg.png)

["Gotta Serve Somebody"](/wiki/File:Gotta_Serve_Somebody.ogg "File:Gotta Serve Somebody.ogg")

Dylan took five months off at the beginning of 1979 to attend Bible school.[[31]](#cite_note-Crowe-1985-31) His subsequent album *[Slow Train Coming](/wiki/Slow_Train_Coming "Slow Train Coming")* reached No. 3 on the US [*Billboard* 200](/wiki/Billboard_200 "Billboard 200") chart and included this [Grammy](/wiki/Grammy "Grammy")-winning song.

---

*Problems playing this file? See [media help](/wiki/Help:Media "Help:Media").*

In the late 1970s, Dylan converted to [Evangelical Christianity](/wiki/Evangelical_Christianity "Evangelical Christianity"),[[194]](#cite_note-200)[[195]](#cite_note-Vineyard-201) undertaking a three-month discipleship course run by the [Association of Vineyard Churches](/wiki/Association_of_Vineyard_Churches "Association of Vineyard Churches").[[196]](#cite_note-202)[[197]](#cite_note-203) He released three albums of contemporary [gospel music](/wiki/Gospel_music "Gospel music"). *[Slow Train Coming](/wiki/Slow_Train_Coming "Slow Train Coming")* (1979) featured [Dire Straits](/wiki/Dire_Straits "Dire Straits") guitarist [Mark Knopfler](/wiki/Mark_Knopfler "Mark Knopfler") and was produced by veteran R&B producer [Jerry Wexler](/wiki/Jerry_Wexler "Jerry Wexler"). Wexler said that Dylan had tried to evangelize him during the recording. He replied: "Bob, you're dealing with a 62-year-old Jewish atheist. Let's just make an album."[[198]](#cite_note-204) Dylan won the [Grammy Award for Best Male Rock Vocal Performance](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Best_Male_Rock_Vocal_Performance "Grammy Award for Best Male Rock Vocal Performance") for the song "[Gotta Serve Somebody](/wiki/Gotta_Serve_Somebody "Gotta Serve Somebody")". When touring in late 1979 and early 1980, Dylan would not play his older, secular works, and he delivered declarations of his faith from the stage, such as:

> Years ago they ... said I was a prophet. I used to say, "No I'm not a prophet", they say "Yes you are, you're a prophet." I said, "No it's not me." They used to say "You sure are a prophet." They used to convince me I was a prophet. Now I come out and say Jesus Christ is the answer. They say, "Bob Dylan's no prophet." They just can't handle it.[[199]](#cite_note-205)

Dylan's Christianity was unpopular with some fans and musicians.[[200]](#cite_note-206) John Lennon, shortly before [being murdered](/wiki/Murder_of_John_Lennon "Murder of John Lennon"), recorded "Serve Yourself" in response to "Gotta Serve Somebody".[[201]](#cite_note-207) In 1981, [Stephen Holden](/wiki/Stephen_Holden "Stephen Holden") wrote in *The New York Times* that "neither age (he's now 40) nor his much-publicized conversion to born-again Christianity has altered his essentially iconoclastic temperament".[[202]](#cite_note-208)

### 1980–1989: Career fluctuations

In late 1980, Dylan briefly played concerts billed as "A Musical Retrospective", restoring popular 1960s songs to the repertoire. His second Christian album, *[Saved](/wiki/Saved_(Bob_Dylan_album) "Saved (Bob Dylan album)")* (1980), received mixed reviews, described by Michael Gray as "the nearest thing to a follow-up album Dylan has ever made, *Slow Train Coming II* and inferior".[[203]](#cite_note-209) His third Christian album was *[Shot of Love](/wiki/Shot_of_Love "Shot of Love")* (1981).[[204]](#cite_note-Thompson2000-210) The album featured his first secular compositions in more than two years, mixed with Christian songs. The lyrics of "[Every Grain of Sand](/wiki/Every_Grain_of_Sand_(Bob_Dylan_song) "Every Grain of Sand (Bob Dylan song)")" recall [William Blake](/wiki/William_Blake "William Blake")'s "[Auguries of Innocence](/wiki/Auguries_of_Innocence "Auguries of Innocence")".[[205]](#cite_note-211) [Elvis Costello](/wiki/Elvis_Costello "Elvis Costello") wrote that "*Shot of Love* may not be your favorite Bob Dylan record, but it might contain his best song: 'Every Grain of Sand'."[[206]](#cite_note-212)

[![Dylan, onstage and with eyes closed, plays a chord on an electric guitar.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Bob_Dylan_in_Toronto2.jpg/250px-Bob_Dylan_in_Toronto2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bob_Dylan_in_Toronto2.jpg)

Dylan in Toronto, April 18, 1980

Reception of Dylan's 1980s recordings varied. Gray criticized Dylan's 1980s albums for carelessness in the studio and for failing to release his best songs.[[207]](#cite_note-213) *[Infidels](/wiki/Infidels_(album) "Infidels (album)")* (1983) employed Knopfler again as lead guitarist and also as producer; the sessions resulted in several songs that Dylan left off the album. Best regarded of these were "[Blind Willie McTell](/wiki/Blind_Willie_McTell_(song) "Blind Willie McTell (song)")", which was both a tribute to the [eponymous blues musician](/wiki/Blind_Willie_McTell "Blind Willie McTell") and an evocation of [African American history](/wiki/African_American_history "African American history"),[[208]](#cite_note-214) "Foot of Pride" and "[Lord Protect My Child](/wiki/Lord_Protect_My_Child "Lord Protect My Child")". These three songs were later released on *[The Bootleg Series Volumes 1–3 (Rare & Unreleased) 1961–1991](/wiki/The_Bootleg_Series_Volumes_1%E2%80%933_(Rare_%26_Unreleased)_1961%E2%80%931991 "The Bootleg Series Volumes 1–3 (Rare & Unreleased) 1961–1991")*.[[209]](#cite_note-215)

Between July 1984 and March 1985, Dylan recorded *[Empire Burlesque](/wiki/Empire_Burlesque "Empire Burlesque")*.[[210]](#cite_note-Sounes362-216) [Arthur Baker](/wiki/Arthur_Baker_(musician) "Arthur Baker (musician)"), who had remixed hits for Bruce Springsteen and [Cyndi Lauper](/wiki/Cyndi_Lauper "Cyndi Lauper"), was asked to engineer and mix the album. Baker said he felt he was hired to make Dylan's album sound "a little bit more contemporary".[[210]](#cite_note-Sounes362-216) In 1985 Dylan sang on [USA for Africa](/wiki/USA_for_Africa "USA for Africa")'s famine relief single "[We Are the World](/wiki/We_Are_the_World "We Are the World")". He also joined [Artists United Against Apartheid](/wiki/Artists_United_Against_Apartheid "Artists United Against Apartheid"), providing vocals for their single "[Sun City](/wiki/Sun_City_(song) "Sun City (song)")".[[211]](#cite_note-217) On July 13, 1985, he appeared at the [Live Aid](/wiki/Live_Aid "Live Aid") concert at [JFK Stadium](/wiki/JFK_Stadium "JFK Stadium"), Philadelphia. Backed by [Keith Richards](/wiki/Keith_Richards "Keith Richards") and [Ronnie Wood](/wiki/Ronnie_Wood "Ronnie Wood"), he performed a ragged version of "Ballad of Hollis Brown", a tale of rural poverty, and then said to the worldwide audience: "I hope that some of the money ... maybe they can just take a little bit of it, maybe ... one or two million, maybe ... and use it to pay the mortgages on some of the farms and, the farmers here, owe to the banks".[[212]](#cite_note-218) His remarks were widely criticized as inappropriate, but inspired [Willie Nelson](/wiki/Willie_Nelson "Willie Nelson") to organize a concert, [Farm Aid](/wiki/Farm_Aid "Farm Aid"), to benefit debt-ridden American farmers.[[213]](#cite_note-219)

In October 1985, Dylan released *[Biograph](/wiki/Biograph_(album) "Biograph (album)")*, a box set featuring 53 tracks, 18 of them previously unreleased. [Stephen Thomas Erlewine](/wiki/Stephen_Thomas_Erlewine "Stephen Thomas Erlewine") wrote: "Historically, *Biograph* is significant not for what it did for Dylan's career, but for establishing the box set, complete with hits and rarities, as a viable part of rock history."[[214]](#cite_note-220) *Biograph* also contained liner notes by [Cameron Crowe](/wiki/Cameron_Crowe "Cameron Crowe") in which Dylan discussed the origins of some of his songs.[[215]](#cite_note-221)

In April 1986, Dylan made a foray into [rap](/wiki/Rap "Rap") when he added vocals to the opening verse of "Street Rock" on [Kurtis Blow](/wiki/Kurtis_Blow "Kurtis Blow")'s album *[Kingdom Blow](/wiki/Kingdom_Blow "Kingdom Blow")*.[[216]](#cite_note-222) Dylan's next studio album, *[Knocked Out Loaded](/wiki/Knocked_Out_Loaded "Knocked Out Loaded")* (1986), contained three covers (by [Junior Parker](/wiki/Junior_Parker "Junior Parker"), [Kris Kristofferson](/wiki/Kris_Kristofferson "Kris Kristofferson") and the gospel hymn "[Precious Memories](/wiki/Precious_Memories_(hymn) "Precious Memories (hymn)")"), plus three collaborations (with [Tom Petty](/wiki/Tom_Petty "Tom Petty"), Sam Shepard and [Carole Bayer Sager](/wiki/Carole_Bayer_Sager "Carole Bayer Sager")), and two solo compositions by Dylan. A reviewer wrote:

> the record follows too many detours to be consistently compelling, and some of those detours wind down roads that are indisputably dead ends. By 1986, such uneven records weren't entirely unexpected by Dylan, but that didn't make them any less frustrating.[[217]](#cite_note-223)

It was the first Dylan album since his 1962 debut to fail to make the Top 50.[[218]](#cite_note-224) Some critics have called the song Dylan co-wrote with Shepard, "[Brownsville Girl](/wiki/Brownsville_Girl "Brownsville Girl")", a masterpiece.[[219]](#cite_note-225)

In 1986 and 1987, Dylan toured with [Tom Petty and the Heartbreakers](/wiki/Tom_Petty_and_the_Heartbreakers "Tom Petty and the Heartbreakers"), sharing vocals with Petty on several songs each night. Dylan also toured with the [Grateful Dead](/wiki/Grateful_Dead "Grateful Dead") in 1987, resulting in the live album *[Dylan & The Dead](/wiki/Dylan_%26_The_Dead "Dylan & The Dead")*, which received negative reviews; Erlewine said it was "quite possibly the worst album by either Bob Dylan or the Grateful Dead".[[220]](#cite_note-226) Dylan initiated what came to be called the [Never Ending Tour](/wiki/Never_Ending_Tour "Never Ending Tour") on June 7, 1988, performing with a back-up band featuring guitarist [G. E. Smith](/wiki/G._E._Smith "G. E. Smith"). Dylan would continue to tour with a small, changing band for the next 30 years.[[221]](#cite_note-227) In 1987, Dylan starred in [Richard Marquand](/wiki/Richard_Marquand "Richard Marquand")'s movie *[Hearts of Fire](/wiki/Hearts_of_Fire "Hearts of Fire")*, in which he played Billy Parker, a washed-up rock star turned chicken farmer whose teenage lover ([Fiona](/wiki/Fiona_(singer) "Fiona (singer)")) leaves him for a jaded English synth-pop sensation ([Rupert Everett](/wiki/Rupert_Everett "Rupert Everett")).[[222]](#cite_note-228) Dylan also contributed two original songs to the soundtrack—"Night After Night", and "Had a Dream About You, Baby", as well as a cover of [John Hiatt](/wiki/John_Hiatt "John Hiatt")'s "The Usual". The film was a critical and commercial flop.[[223]](#cite_note-229)

[![Dylan plays his guitar and sings into a microphone onstage.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Bob_Dylan_Barcelona.jpg/250px-Bob_Dylan_Barcelona.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bob_Dylan_Barcelona.jpg)

Dylan in Barcelona, Spain, 1984

Dylan was inducted into the [Rock and Roll Hall of Fame](/wiki/Rock_and_Roll_Hall_of_Fame "Rock and Roll Hall of Fame") in January 1988. Bruce Springsteen, in his introduction, declared, "Bob freed your mind the way Elvis freed your body. He showed us that just because music was innately physical did not mean that it was anti-intellectual".[[109]](#cite_note-Springsteen-113) *[Down in the Groove](/wiki/Down_in_the_Groove "Down in the Groove")* (1988) sold even more poorly than *Knocked Out Loaded*.[[224]](#cite_note-Sounes_385-230) Gray wrote: "The very title undercuts any idea that inspired work may lie within. Here was a further devaluing of the notion of a new Bob Dylan album as something significant."[[225]](#cite_note-Gray_2000,_p._13-231) The critical and commercial disappointment of that album was swiftly followed by the success of the [Traveling Wilburys](/wiki/Traveling_Wilburys "Traveling Wilburys"), a [supergroup](/wiki/Supergroup_(music) "Supergroup (music)") Dylan co-founded with George Harrison, [Jeff Lynne](/wiki/Jeff_Lynne "Jeff Lynne"), [Roy Orbison](/wiki/Roy_Orbison "Roy Orbison") and Tom Petty. In late 1988, their *[Traveling Wilburys Vol. 1](/wiki/Traveling_Wilburys_Vol._1 "Traveling Wilburys Vol. 1")* reached number three on the US albums chart,[[224]](#cite_note-Sounes_385-230) featuring songs described as Dylan's most accessible in years.[[226]](#cite_note-232) Despite Orbison's death in December 1988, the remaining four recorded a second album in May 1990, *[Traveling Wilburys Vol. 3](/wiki/Traveling_Wilburys_Vol._3 "Traveling Wilburys Vol. 3")*.[[227]](#cite_note-233)

Dylan finished the decade on a critical high note with *[Oh Mercy](/wiki/Oh_Mercy "Oh Mercy")*, produced by [Daniel Lanois](/wiki/Daniel_Lanois "Daniel Lanois"). Gray praised the album as "Attentively written, vocally distinctive, musically warm, and uncompromisingly professional, this cohesive whole is the nearest thing to a great Bob Dylan album in the 1980s."[[225]](#cite_note-Gray_2000,_p._13-231) "[Most of the Time](/wiki/Most_of_the_Time "Most of the Time")", a lost-love composition, was prominently featured in the film *[High Fidelity](/wiki/High_Fidelity_(film) "High Fidelity (film)")* (2000), while "[What Was It You Wanted](/wiki/What_Was_It_You_Wanted "What Was It You Wanted")" has been interpreted both as a catechism and a wry comment on the expectations of critics and fans.[[228]](#cite_note-234) The religious imagery of "[Ring Them Bells](/wiki/Ring_Them_Bells_(song) "Ring Them Bells (song)")" struck some critics as a re-affirmation of faith.[[229]](#cite_note-235)

### 1990–1999: Return to folk music and resurgence

Dylan's 1990s began with *[Under the Red Sky](/wiki/Under_the_Red_Sky "Under the Red Sky")* (1990), an about-face from the serious *Oh Mercy*. It contained several apparently simple songs, including "Under the Red Sky" and "Wiggle Wiggle". The album was dedicated to "Gabby Goo Goo", a nickname for the daughter of Dylan and [Carolyn Dennis](/wiki/Carolyn_Dennis "Carolyn Dennis"), Desiree Gabrielle Dennis-Dylan, who was four.[[230]](#cite_note-236) Musicians on the album included George Harrison, [Slash](/wiki/Slash_(musician) "Slash (musician)"), [David Crosby](/wiki/David_Crosby "David Crosby"), [Bruce Hornsby](/wiki/Bruce_Hornsby "Bruce Hornsby"), [Stevie Ray Vaughan](/wiki/Stevie_Ray_Vaughan "Stevie Ray Vaughan"), and [Elton John](/wiki/Elton_John "Elton John"). The record received negative reviews and sold poorly.[[231]](#cite_note-237) In 1990 and 1991 Dylan was described by his biographers as drinking heavily, impairing his performances on stage.[[232]](#cite_note-238)[[233]](#cite_note-239) In an interview with *Rolling Stone*, Dylan dismissed allegations that drinking was interfering with his music: "That's completely inaccurate. I can drink or not drink. I don't know why people would associate drinking with *anything* I do, really".[[234]](#cite_note-240)

Defilement and remorse were themes Dylan addressed when he received a [Grammy Lifetime Achievement Award](/wiki/Grammy_Lifetime_Achievement_Award "Grammy Lifetime Achievement Award") from [Jack Nicholson](/wiki/Jack_Nicholson "Jack Nicholson") in February 1991.[[235]](#cite_note-Lifetime-241) The event coincided with the start of the [Gulf War](/wiki/Gulf_War "Gulf War") and Dylan played "[Masters of War](/wiki/Masters_of_War "Masters of War")"; *Rolling Stone* called his performance "almost unintelligible".[[236]](#cite_note-R&REncylopedia-242) He made a short speech: "My daddy once said to me, he said, 'Son, it is possible for you to become so defiled in this world that your own mother and father will abandon you.
