If that happens, God will believe in your ability to mend your own ways'".[[235]](#cite_note-Lifetime-241)[[237]](#cite_note-Heylin-664-243) This was a paraphrase of 19th-century Orthodox Rabbi [Samson Raphael Hirsch](/wiki/Samson_Raphael_Hirsch "Samson Raphael Hirsch")'s commentary on Psalm 27.[[238]](#cite_note-244) On October 16, 1992, the thirtieth anniversary of Dylan's debut album was celebrated with a concert at [Madison Square Garden](/wiki/Madison_Square_Garden "Madison Square Garden"), christened "Bobfest" by Neil Young and featuring [John Mellencamp](/wiki/John_Mellencamp "John Mellencamp"), [Stevie Wonder](/wiki/Stevie_Wonder "Stevie Wonder"), [Lou Reed](/wiki/Lou_Reed "Lou Reed"), [Eddie Vedder](/wiki/Eddie_Vedder "Eddie Vedder"), Dylan and others. It was recorded as the live album *[The 30th Anniversary Concert Celebration](/wiki/The_30th_Anniversary_Concert_Celebration "The 30th Anniversary Concert Celebration")*.[[236]](#cite_note-R&REncylopedia-242)

Over the next few years Dylan returned to his roots with two albums covering traditional folk and blues songs: *[Good as I Been to You](/wiki/Good_as_I_Been_to_You "Good as I Been to You")* (1992) and *[World Gone Wrong](/wiki/World_Gone_Wrong "World Gone Wrong")* (1993), backed solely by his acoustic guitar.[[239]](#cite_note-245) Many critics and fans noted the quiet beauty of the song "Lone Pilgrim",[[240]](#cite_note-246) written by a 19th-century teacher. In August 1994, he played at [Woodstock '94](/wiki/Woodstock_%2794 "Woodstock '94"); *Rolling Stone* called his performance "triumphant".[[236]](#cite_note-R&REncylopedia-242) In November, Dylan recorded two live shows for *[MTV Unplugged](/wiki/MTV_Unplugged "MTV Unplugged")*. He said his wish to perform traditional songs was overruled by [Sony](/wiki/Sony "Sony") executives who insisted on hits.[[241]](#cite_note-247) The resulting album, *[MTV Unplugged](/wiki/MTV_Unplugged_(Bob_Dylan_album) "MTV Unplugged (Bob Dylan album)")*, included ["John Brown"](/wiki/John_Brown_(song) "John Brown (song)"), an unreleased 1962 song about how enthusiasm for war ends in mutilation and disillusionment.[[242]](#cite_note-248)

[![Dylan and members of his band perform onstage. Dylan, wearing a red shirt and black pants, plays an electric guitar and sings.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Bob_Dylan_1996.jpg/250px-Bob_Dylan_1996.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bob_Dylan_1996.jpg)

Dylan performs during the 1996 Lida Festival in [Stockholm](/wiki/Stockholm "Stockholm")

With a collection of songs reportedly written while snowed in on his Minnesota ranch,[[243]](#cite_note-249) Dylan booked recording time with Daniel Lanois at Miami's [Criteria Studios](/wiki/Criteria_Studios "Criteria Studios") in January 1997. The subsequent recording sessions were, by some accounts, fraught with musical tension.[[244]](#cite_note-250) Before the album's release Dylan was hospitalized with life-threatening [pericarditis](/wiki/Pericarditis "Pericarditis"), brought on by [histoplasmosis](/wiki/Histoplasmosis "Histoplasmosis"). His scheduled European tour was canceled, but Dylan made a speedy recovery and left the hospital saying, "I really thought I'd be seeing Elvis soon".[[245]](#cite_note-251) He was back on the road by mid-year, and performed before [Pope John Paul II](/wiki/Pope_John_Paul_II "Pope John Paul II") at the World Eucharistic Conference in [Bologna](/wiki/Bologna "Bologna"), Italy. The Pope treated the audience of 200,000 to a [homily](/wiki/Homily "Homily") based on Dylan's "Blowin' in the Wind".[[246]](#cite_note-252)

In September, Dylan released the new Lanois-produced album, *[Time Out of Mind](/wiki/Time_Out_of_Mind_(Bob_Dylan_album) "Time Out of Mind (Bob Dylan album)")*. With its bitter assessments of love and morbid ruminations, Dylan's first collection of original songs in seven years was highly acclaimed. [Alex Ross](/wiki/Alex_Ross_(music_critic) "Alex Ross (music critic)") called it "a thrilling return to form".[[247]](#cite_note-Ross-253) "[Cold Irons Bound](/wiki/Cold_Irons_Bound "Cold Irons Bound")" won Dylan another Grammy For Best Male Rock Vocal Performance, and the album won him his first [Grammy Award for Album of the Year](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Album_of_the_Year "Grammy Award for Album of the Year").[[248]](#cite_note-254) The album's first single, "[Not Dark Yet](/wiki/Not_Dark_Yet "Not Dark Yet")", has been called one of Dylan's best songs[[249]](#cite_note-255) and "[Make You Feel My Love](/wiki/Make_You_Feel_My_Love "Make You Feel My Love")" was covered by [Billy Joel](/wiki/Billy_Joel "Billy Joel"), [Garth Brooks](/wiki/Garth_Brooks "Garth Brooks"), [Adele](/wiki/Adele "Adele") and others. Elvis Costello said "I think it might be the best record he's made."[[250]](#cite_note-256)

### 2000–2009: Oscar win, memoir, and *Modern Times*

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Gnome-mime-audio-openclipart.svg/60px-Gnome-mime-audio-openclipart.svg.png)

["Things Have Changed"](/wiki/File:Things_Have_Changed.ogg "File:Things Have Changed.ogg")

Dylan's [Oscar](/wiki/Academy_Award "Academy Award")-winning song was featured in the movie *[Wonder Boys](/wiki/Wonder_Boys_(film) "Wonder Boys (film)")*. The line "sapphire-tinted skies" echoes the verse of [Shelley](/wiki/Percy_Bysshe_Shelley "Percy Bysshe Shelley")[[251]](#cite_note-257) while "forty miles of bad road" echoes [Duane Eddy](/wiki/Duane_Eddy "Duane Eddy")'s [hit single](/wiki/Forty_Miles_of_Bad_Road "Forty Miles of Bad Road").

---

*Problems playing this file? See [media help](/wiki/Help:Media "Help:Media").*

In 2001, Dylan won an [Academy Award for Best Original Song](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Original_Song "Academy Award for Best Original Song") for "[Things Have Changed](/wiki/Things_Have_Changed "Things Have Changed")", written for the film *[Wonder Boys](/wiki/Wonder_Boys_(film) "Wonder Boys (film)")*.[[252]](#cite_note-258) *["Love and Theft"](/wiki/%22Love_and_Theft%22 "\"Love and Theft\"")* was released on September 11, 2001. Recorded with his touring band, Dylan produced the album under the alias Jack Frost.[[253]](#cite_note-259) Critics noted that Dylan was widening his musical palette to include [rockabilly](/wiki/Rockabilly "Rockabilly"), [Western swing](/wiki/Western_swing "Western swing"), [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") and [lounge music](/wiki/Lounge_music "Lounge music").[[254]](#cite_note-260) The album won the [Grammy Award for Best Contemporary Folk Album](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Best_Contemporary_Folk_Album "Grammy Award for Best Contemporary Folk Album").[[255]](#cite_note-261) Controversy ensued when *[The Wall Street Journal](/wiki/The_Wall_Street_Journal "The Wall Street Journal")* pointed out similarities between the album's lyrics and [Junichi Saga](/wiki/Junichi_Saga "Junichi Saga")'s book *[Confessions of a Yakuza](/wiki/Confessions_of_a_Yakuza "Confessions of a Yakuza")*. Saga was not familiar with Dylan's work, but said he was flattered. Upon hearing the album, Saga said of Dylan: "His lines flow from one image to the next and don't always make sense. But they have a great atmosphere."[[256]](#cite_note-262)[[257]](#cite_note-263)

In 2003, Dylan revisited the evangelical songs from his Christian period and participated in the project *[Gotta Serve Somebody: The Gospel Songs of Bob Dylan](/wiki/Gotta_Serve_Somebody:_The_Gospel_Songs_of_Bob_Dylan "Gotta Serve Somebody: The Gospel Songs of Bob Dylan")*. That year, Dylan released *[Masked & Anonymous](/wiki/Masked_%26_Anonymous "Masked & Anonymous")*, which he co-wrote with director [Larry Charles](/wiki/Larry_Charles "Larry Charles") under the alias Sergei Petrov.[[258]](#cite_note-264) Dylan starred as Jack Fate, alongside a cast that included [Jeff Bridges](/wiki/Jeff_Bridges "Jeff Bridges"), [Penélope Cruz](/wiki/Pen%C3%A9lope_Cruz "Penélope Cruz") and [John Goodman](/wiki/John_Goodman "John Goodman"). The film polarized critics.[[259]](#cite_note-265) In *The New York Times*, [A. O. Scott](/wiki/A._O._Scott "A. O. Scott") called it as an "incoherent mess";[[260]](#cite_note-266) a few treated it as a serious work of art.[[261]](#cite_note-267)[[262]](#cite_note-Masked-268)

In 2004, Dylan published the first part of his memoir, *[Chronicles: Volume One](/wiki/Chronicles:_Volume_One "Chronicles: Volume One")*. Confounding expectations,[[263]](#cite_note-Maslin-269) Dylan devoted three chapters to his first year in New York City in 1961–1962, virtually ignoring the mid-1960s when his fame was at its height, while devoting chapters to the albums *New Morning* (1970) and *Oh Mercy* (1989). The book reached number two on *The New York Times'* Hardcover Non-Fiction bestseller list in December 2004 and was nominated for a [National Book Award](/wiki/National_Book_Award "National Book Award").[[264]](#cite_note-270)

Critics noted that *Chronicles* contained many examples of pastiche and borrowing; sources included [*Time* magazine](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)")[[265]](#cite_note-271) and the novels of [Jack London](/wiki/Jack_London "Jack London").[[266]](#cite_note-272)
Biographer [Clinton Heylin](/wiki/Clinton_Heylin "Clinton Heylin") queried the veracity of Dylan's autobiography, noting "Not a single checkable story held water; not one anecdote couldn't be shot full of holes by any half-decent researcher."[[267]](#cite_note-273)

Martin Scorsese's Dylan documentary *[No Direction Home](/wiki/No_Direction_Home "No Direction Home")* was broadcast on September 26–27, 2005, on [BBC Two](/wiki/BBC_Two "BBC Two") in the UK and as part of *[American Masters](/wiki/American_Masters "American Masters")* on [PBS](/wiki/PBS "PBS") in the US.[[268]](#cite_note-274) It covers the period from Dylan's arrival in New York in 1961 to his motorcycle crash in 1966, featuring interviews with [Suze Rotolo](/wiki/Suze_Rotolo "Suze Rotolo"), [Liam Clancy](/wiki/Liam_Clancy "Liam Clancy"), Joan Baez, Allen Ginsberg, Pete Seeger, [Mavis Staples](/wiki/Mavis_Staples "Mavis Staples") and Dylan himself. The film earned a [Peabody Award](/wiki/Peabody_Award "Peabody Award")[[269]](#cite_note-275) and a [Columbia-duPont Award](/wiki/Alfred_I._duPont%E2%80%93Columbia_University_Award "Alfred I. duPont–Columbia University Award").[[270]](#cite_note-276) The [accompanying soundtrack](/wiki/The_Bootleg_Series_Vol._7:_No_Direction_Home:_The_Soundtrack "The Bootleg Series Vol. 7: No Direction Home: The Soundtrack") featured unreleased songs from Dylan's early years.[[271]](#cite_note-NYT-277)

Dylan's career as a radio presenter began on May 3, 2006, with his weekly program, *[Theme Time Radio Hour](/wiki/Theme_Time_Radio_Hour "Theme Time Radio Hour")*, on [XM Satellite Radio](/wiki/XM_Satellite_Radio "XM Satellite Radio"). He played songs with a common theme, such as "Weather", "Weddings", "Dance" and "Dreams".[[272]](#cite_note-278)[[273]](#cite_note-279) Dylan's records ranged from [Muddy Waters](/wiki/Muddy_Waters "Muddy Waters") to [Prince](/wiki/Prince_(musician) "Prince (musician)"), [L.L. Cool J](/wiki/L.L._Cool_J "L.L. Cool J") to [the Streets](/wiki/The_Streets "The Streets"). Dylan's show was praised for the breadth of his musical selections[[274]](#cite_note-280) and for his jokes, stories and eclectic references.[[275]](#cite_note-281)[[276]](#cite_note-282) In April 2009, Dylan broadcast the 100th show in his radio series; the theme was "Goodbye" and he signed off with Woody Guthrie's "[So Long, It's Been Good to Know Yuh](/wiki/So_Long,_It%27s_Been_Good_to_Know_Yuh "So Long, It's Been Good to Know Yuh")".[[277]](#cite_note-283)

[![Dylan together with five members of his band onstage. Dylan, dressed in a white shirt and black pants, is second from right.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Dylan2_Spectrum.jpg/250px-Dylan2_Spectrum.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dylan2_Spectrum.jpg)

Dylan at the Spectrum, 2007

Dylan released *[Modern Times](/wiki/Modern_Times_(Bob_Dylan_album) "Modern Times (Bob Dylan album)")* in August 2006. Despite some coarsening of Dylan's voice (a critic for *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")* characterized his singing on the album as "a catarrhal death rattle"[[278]](#cite_note-284)) most reviewers praised the album, and many described it as the final installment of a successful trilogy, encompassing *Time Out of Mind* and *"Love and Theft"*.[[279]](#cite_note-285) *Modern Times* entered the US charts at number one, making it Dylan's first album to reach that position since 1976's *Desire*.[[280]](#cite_note-286) *The New York Times* published an article exploring similarities between some of Dylan's lyrics in *Modern Times* and the work of the [Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War") poet [Henry Timrod](/wiki/Henry_Timrod "Henry Timrod").[[281]](#cite_note-nytTimrod-287) *Modern Times* won the Grammy Award for Best Contemporary Folk Album and Dylan won [Best Solo Rock Vocal Performance](/wiki/Best_Solo_Rock_Vocal_Performance "Best Solo Rock Vocal Performance") for "Someday Baby".[[282]](#cite_note-288) *Modern Times* was named Album of the Year by *Rolling Stone*[[283]](#cite_note-289) and *[Uncut](/wiki/Uncut_(magazine) "Uncut (magazine)")*.[[284]](#cite_note-290) On the same day that *Modern Times* was released, the [iTunes Music Store](/wiki/ITunes_Music_Store "ITunes Music Store") released *[Bob Dylan: The Collection](/wiki/Bob_Dylan:_The_Collection "Bob Dylan: The Collection")*, a digital box set containing all of his albums (773 tracks), along with 42 rare and unreleased tracks.[[285]](#cite_note-291)

[![Dylan, dressed in a black western outfit with red highlights, stands onstage and plays the keyboards. He gazes to the left of the photo. Behind him is a guitar player, dressed in black.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/Bob_Dylan_in_Toronto.jpg/250px-Bob_Dylan_in_Toronto.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bob_Dylan_in_Toronto.jpg)

Dylan performs at Air Canada Centre, Toronto, November 7, 2006

On October 1, 2007, Columbia Records released the triple CD retrospective *[Dylan](/wiki/Dylan_(2007_album) "Dylan (2007 album)")*, anthologizing his entire career under the *Dylan 07* logo.[[286]](#cite_note-292) The sophistication of the *Dylan 07* marketing campaign was a reminder that Dylan's commercial profile had risen considerably since the 1990s. This became evident in 2004, when Dylan appeared in a TV advertisement for [Victoria's Secret](/wiki/Victoria%27s_Secret "Victoria's Secret").[[287]](#cite_note-293) In October 2007, he participated in a multi-media campaign for the 2008 [Cadillac Escalade](/wiki/Cadillac_Escalade "Cadillac Escalade").[[288]](#cite_note-294)[[289]](#cite_note-295) In 2009 he gave the highest profile endorsement of his career to date, appearing with rapper [will.i.am](/wiki/Will.i.am "Will.i.am") in a [Pepsi](/wiki/Pepsi "Pepsi") ad that debuted during [Super Bowl XLIII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLIII "Super Bowl XLIII"). The ad opened with Dylan singing the first verse of "Forever Young" followed by will.i.am doing a [hip hop](/wiki/Hip_hop "Hip hop") version of the song's final verse.[[290]](#cite_note-296)

*[The Bootleg Series Vol. 8 – Tell Tale Signs](/wiki/The_Bootleg_Series_Vol._8_%E2%80%93_Tell_Tale_Signs:_Rare_and_Unreleased_1989%E2%80%932006 "The Bootleg Series Vol. 8 – Tell Tale Signs: Rare and Unreleased 1989–2006")* was released in October 2008, as both a two-CD set and a three-CD version with a 150-page hardcover book. The set contains live performances and outtakes from selected studio albums from *Oh Mercy* to *Modern Times*, as well as soundtrack contributions and collaborations with [David Bromberg](/wiki/David_Bromberg "David Bromberg") and [Ralph Stanley](/wiki/Ralph_Stanley "Ralph Stanley").[[291]](#cite_note-297) The pricing of the album—the two-CD set went on sale for $18.99 and the three-CD version for $129.99—led to complaints about "rip-off packaging".[[292]](#cite_note-298)[[293]](#cite_note-299) The release was widely acclaimed by critics.[[294]](#cite_note-300) The abundance of alternative takes and unreleased material suggested to one reviewer that this volume of old outtakes "feels like a new Bob Dylan record, not only for the astonishing freshness of the material, but also for the incredible sound quality and organic feeling of everything here".[[295]](#cite_note-301)

Dylan released *[Together Through Life](/wiki/Together_Through_Life "Together Through Life")* on April 28, 2009. In a conversation with music journalist Bill Flanagan, Dylan explained it originated when French director [Olivier Dahan](/wiki/Olivier_Dahan "Olivier Dahan") asked him to supply a song for his movie *[My Own Love Song](/wiki/My_Own_Love_Song "My Own Love Song")*. He initially intended to record a single track, "Life Is Hard", but "the record sort of took its own direction".[[296]](#cite_note-302) Nine of the album's ten songs are credited as co-written by Dylan and [Robert Hunter](/wiki/Robert_Hunter_(lyricist) "Robert Hunter (lyricist)").[[297]](#cite_note-303) The album received largely favorable reviews,[[298]](#cite_note-304) although several critics described it as a minor addition to Dylan's canon.[[299]](#cite_note-305) In its first week of release, the album reached number one on the [*Billboard* 200](/wiki/Billboard_200 "Billboard 200") chart in the US, making Dylan, at 67 years of age, the oldest artist to ever debut at number one on that chart.[[300]](#cite_note-Caulfield,_Keith-306)

Dylan's *[Christmas in the Heart](/wiki/Christmas_in_the_Heart "Christmas in the Heart")* was released in October 2009, comprising such [Christmas standards](/wiki/Christmas_music "Christmas music") as "[Little Drummer Boy](/wiki/Little_Drummer_Boy "Little Drummer Boy")", "[Winter Wonderland](/wiki/Winter_Wonderland "Winter Wonderland")" and "[Here Comes Santa Claus](/wiki/Here_Comes_Santa_Claus "Here Comes Santa Claus")".[[301]](#cite_note-307) [Edna Gundersen](/wiki/Edna_Gundersen "Edna Gundersen") wrote that Dylan was "revisiting yuletide styles popularized by [Nat King Cole](/wiki/Nat_King_Cole "Nat King Cole"), [Mel Tormé](/wiki/Mel_Torm%C3%A9 "Mel Tormé"), and the [Ray Conniff Singers](/wiki/Ray_Conniff_Singers "Ray Conniff Singers")".[[302]](#cite_note-308) Dylan's royalties from the album were donated to the charities [Feeding America](/wiki/Feeding_America "Feeding America") in the US, [Crisis](/wiki/Crisis_(charity) "Crisis (charity)") in the UK, and the [World Food Programme](/wiki/World_Food_Programme "World Food Programme").[[303]](#cite_note-UK_Fundraising-309) The album received generally favorable reviews.[[304]](#cite_note-310) In an interview published in *[The Big Issue](/wiki/The_Big_Issue "The Big Issue")*, Flanagan asked Dylan why he had performed the songs in a straightforward style, and he replied: "There wasn't any other way to play it. These songs are part of my life, just like folk songs. You have to play them straight too."[[305]](#cite_note-Dylan_2009_interview_with_Bill_Flanagan-311)

### 2010–2019: *Tempest* and continued recordings

Volume 9 of Dylan's Bootleg Series, [*The Witmark Demos*](/wiki/The_Bootleg_Series_Vol._9_%E2%80%93_The_Witmark_Demos:_1962%E2%80%931964 "The Bootleg Series Vol. 9 – The Witmark Demos: 1962–1964"), was issued in October 2010. It comprised [demo recordings](/wiki/Demo_recordings "Demo recordings") of songs taped between 1962-64 for Dylan's earliest music publishers: Leeds Music in 1962, and [Witmark Music](/wiki/M._Witmark_%26_Sons "M. Witmark & Sons") from 1962-64. A reviewer described the set as "a hearty glimpse of young Bob Dylan changing the music business, and the world, one note at a time."[[306]](#cite_note-312) On [Metacritic](/wiki/Metacritic "Metacritic"), the album has a score of 86, indicating "universal acclaim".[[307]](#cite_note-313) [Sony Legacy](/wiki/Sony_Legacy "Sony Legacy") released *[Bob Dylan: The Original Mono Recordings](/wiki/The_Original_Mono_Recordings "The Original Mono Recordings")*, a boxset that presented Dylan's eight earliest albums, from *Bob Dylan* (1962) to *John Wesley Harding* (1967), in their original mono mix.[[308]](#cite_note-314)[[309]](#cite_note-315)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/Dylan-Obamas-White_House-20100209.jpg/250px-Dylan-Obamas-White_House-20100209.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dylan-Obamas-White_House-20100209.jpg)

Dylan and the Obamas at the [White House](/wiki/White_House "White House"), after a performance celebrating music from the [civil rights movement](/wiki/Civil_rights_movement "Civil rights movement") on February 9, 2010

On Dylan's 70th birthday, the universities of [Mainz](/wiki/University_of_Mainz "University of Mainz"),[[310]](#cite_note-316) [Vienna](/wiki/University_of_Vienna "University of Vienna"),[[311]](#cite_note-317) and [Bristol](/wiki/University_of_Bristol "University of Bristol")[[312]](#cite_note-318) invited literary critics and historians to give papers on Dylan's work. Other events, including tribute bands, discussions and singalongs, took place. The Guardian wrote: "From Moscow to Madrid, Norway to Northampton and Malaysia to his home state of Minnesota, self-confessed 'Bobcats' will gather today to celebrate the 70th birthday of a giant of popular music."[[313]](#cite_note-319)

Dylan's 35th studio album, *[Tempest](/wiki/Tempest_(Bob_Dylan_album) "Tempest (Bob Dylan album)")*, was released on September 11, 2012.[[314]](#cite_note-320) The album features a tribute to Lennon, "[Roll On John](/wiki/Roll_On_John "Roll On John")", and [the title track](/wiki/Tempest_(Bob_Dylan_song) "Tempest (Bob Dylan song)") is a 14-minute song about the [sinking of the *Titanic*](/wiki/Sinking_of_the_Titanic "Sinking of the Titanic").[[315]](#cite_note-321) In *Rolling Stone*, Will Hermes gave *Tempest* five out of five stars: "Lyrically, Dylan is at the top of his game, joking around, dropping wordplay and allegories that evade pat readings and quoting other folks' words like a freestyle rapper on fire".[[316]](#cite_note-tempestrollingstone-322)

Volume 10 of Dylan's Bootleg Series, *[Another Self Portrait (1969–1971)](/wiki/The_Bootleg_Series_Vol._10:_Another_Self_Portrait_(1969%E2%80%931971) "The Bootleg Series Vol. 10: Another Self Portrait (1969–1971)")*, was released in August 2013.[[317]](#cite_note-323) The album contained 35 previously unreleased tracks, including alternative takes and demos from Dylan's 1969–71 recording sessions during the making of the *Self Portrait* and *New Morning* albums. The box set included a live recording of Dylan's performance with the Band at the Isle of Wight Festival in 1969. Thom Jurek wrote, "For fans, this is more than a curiosity, it's an indispensable addition to the catalog."[[318]](#cite_note-324) Columbia Records released a boxset containing all 35 studio albums, six albums of live recordings and a collection of non-album material (*Sidetracks*) as *[Bob Dylan: Complete Album Collection: Vol. One](/wiki/The_Complete_Album_Collection_Vol._1_(album) "The Complete Album Collection Vol. 1 (album)")*, in November.[[319]](#cite_note-325)[[320]](#cite_note-326) To publicize the box set, a video of "Like a Rolling Stone" was released on Dylan's website. The interactive video, allowed viewers to switch between 16 simulated TV channels, all featuring characters lip-synching the lyrics.[[321]](#cite_note-327)[[322]](#cite_note-328)

Dylan appeared in a commercial for the [Chrysler 200](/wiki/Chrysler_200 "Chrysler 200") which aired during the [2014 Super Bowl](/wiki/2014_Super_Bowl "2014 Super Bowl"). In it, he says that "Detroit made cars and cars made America... So let Germany brew your beer, let Switzerland make your watch, let Asia assemble your phone. *We* will build your car." Dylan's ad was criticized for its [protectionist](/wiki/Protectionist "Protectionist") implications, and people wondered whether he had "[sold out](/wiki/Sold_out "Sold out")".[[323]](#cite_note-329)[[324]](#cite_note-330) *The Lyrics: Since 1962* was published by [Simon & Schuster](/wiki/Simon_%26_Schuster "Simon & Schuster") in the fall of 2014. The book was edited by literary critic [Christopher Ricks](/wiki/Christopher_Ricks "Christopher Ricks"), Julie Nemrow and Lisa Nemrow and offered variant versions of Dylan's songs, sourced from out-takes and live performances. A limited edition of 50 books, signed by Dylan, was priced at $5,000. "It's the biggest, most expensive book we've ever published, as far as I know", said Jonathan Karp, Simon & Schuster's president and publisher.[[325]](#cite_note-331)[[326]](#cite_note-332) A comprehensive edition of the Basement Tapes, songs recorded by Dylan and the Band in 1967, was released as *[The Bootleg Series Vol. 11: The Basement Tapes Complete](/wiki/The_Bootleg_Series_Vol._11:_The_Basement_Tapes_Complete "The Bootleg Series Vol. 11: The Basement Tapes Complete")* in November 2014. The album included 138 tracks; the 1975 album *The Basement Tapes* contained just 24 tracks from the material which Dylan and the Band had recorded at their homes in Woodstock, New York in 1967. Subsequently, [over 100 recordings and alternate takes](/wiki/List_of_Basement_Tapes_songs "List of Basement Tapes songs") had circulated on bootleg records. The sleeve notes are by author [Sid Griffin](/wiki/Sid_Griffin "Sid Griffin").[[327]](#cite_note-333)[[328]](#cite_note-334) *The Basement Tapes Complete* won the [Grammy Award for Best Historical Album](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Best_Historical_Album "Grammy Award for Best Historical Album").[[329]](#cite_note-335) The box set earned a score of 99 on Metacritic.[[330]](#cite_note-336)

In February 2015, Dylan released *[Shadows in the Night](/wiki/Shadows_in_the_Night "Shadows in the Night")*, featuring ten songs written between 1923 and 1963,[[331]](#cite_note-secret-337)[[332]](#cite_note-ShadowsBauder-338) which have been described as part of the [Great American Songbook](/wiki/Great_American_Songbook "Great American Songbook").[[333]](#cite_note-ShadowsPetridis-339) All the songs had been recorded by [Frank Sinatra](/wiki/Frank_Sinatra "Frank Sinatra"), but critics and Dylan himself cautioned against seeing the record as a collection of "Sinatra covers".[[331]](#cite_note-secret-337)[[334]](#cite_note-ShadowsGq-340) Dylan explained: "I don't see myself as covering these songs in any way. They've been covered enough. Buried, as a matter a fact. What me and my band are basically doing is uncovering them. Lifting them out of the grave and bringing them into the light of day".[[335]](#cite_note-341) Critics praised the restrained instrumental backings and quality of Dylan's singing.[[333]](#cite_note-ShadowsPetridis-339)[[336]](#cite_note-ShadowsTelegraph-342) The album debuted at number one in the [UK Albums Chart](/wiki/UK_Albums_Chart "UK Albums Chart").[[337]](#cite_note-343) *[The Bootleg Series Vol. 12: The Cutting Edge 1965–1966](/wiki/The_Bootleg_Series_Vol._12:_The_Cutting_Edge_1965%E2%80%931966 "The Bootleg Series Vol. 12: The Cutting Edge 1965–1966")*, consisting of previously unreleased material from the three albums Dylan recorded between January 1965 and March 1966 (*Bringing It All Back Home*, *Highway 61 Revisited* and *Blonde on Blonde*) was released in November 2015. On Dylan's website the "Collector's Edition" was described as containing "every single note recorded by Bob Dylan in the studio in 1965/1966".[[338]](#cite_note-344)[[339]](#cite_note-345)[[340]](#cite_note-346)

Dylan released *[Fallen Angels](/wiki/Fallen_Angels_(Bob_Dylan_album) "Fallen Angels (Bob Dylan album)")*, described as "a direct continuation of the work of 'uncovering' the Great Songbook that he began on *Shadows In the Night*", in May.[[341]](#cite_note-Ward-347) The album contained twelve songs by classic songwriters such as [Harold Arlen](/wiki/Harold_Arlen "Harold Arlen"), [Sammy Cahn](/wiki/Sammy_Cahn "Sammy Cahn") and [Johnny Mercer](/wiki/Johnny_Mercer "Johnny Mercer"), eleven of which had been recorded by Sinatra.[[341]](#cite_note-Ward-347) Jim Farber wrote: "Tellingly, [Dylan] delivers these songs of love lost and cherished not with a burning passion but with the wistfulness of experience. They're memory songs now, intoned with a present sense of commitment. Released just four days ahead of his 75th birthday, they couldn't be more age-appropriate".[[342]](#cite_note-348) *[The 1966 Live Recordings](/wiki/The_1966_Live_Recordings "The 1966 Live Recordings")*, including every known recording of Dylan's 1966 concert tour, was released in November 2016.[[343]](#cite_note-349) The recordings commence with the concert in White Plains New York on February 5, 1966, and end with the [Royal Albert Hall](/wiki/Royal_Albert_Hall "Royal Albert Hall") concert in London on May 27.[[344]](#cite_note-350)[[345]](#cite_note-351) *The New York Times* reported most of the concerts had "never been heard in any form", and described the set as "a monumental addition to the corpus".[[346]](#cite_note-352)

In March 2017, Dylan released a triple album of 30 recordings of classic American songs, *[Triplicate](/wiki/Triplicate_(Bob_Dylan_album) "Triplicate (Bob Dylan album)")*. Dylan's 38th studio album was recorded in [Hollywood's Capitol Studios](/wiki/Capitol_Studios "Capitol Studios") and features his touring band.[[347]](#cite_note-353) Dylan posted an interview on his website to promote the album, and was asked if this material was an exercise in nostalgia.

> Nostalgic? No I wouldn't say that. It's not taking a trip down memory lane or longing and yearning for the good old days or fond memories of what's no more. A song like ['Sentimental Journey'](/wiki/Sentimental_Journey_(song) "Sentimental Journey (song)") is not a way back when song, it doesn't emulate the past, it's attainable and down to earth, it's in the here and now.[[348]](#cite_note-354)

Critics praised the thoroughness of Dylan's exploration of the Great American Songbook, though, in the opinion of *Uncut*, "For all its easy charms, *Triplicate* labours its point to the brink of overkill. After five albums' worth of croon toons, this feels like a fat full stop on a fascinating chapter."[[349]](#cite_note-355)
The next volume of Dylan's Bootleg Series revisited his "Born Again" Christian period of 1979 to 1981, described by *Rolling Stone* as "an intense, wildly controversial time that produced three albums and some of the most confrontational concerts of his long career".[[350]](#cite_note-Trouble-356) Reviewing the box set *[The Bootleg Series Vol. 13: Trouble No More 1979–1981](/wiki/The_Bootleg_Series_Vol._13:_Trouble_No_More_1979%E2%80%931981 "The Bootleg Series Vol. 13: Trouble No More 1979–1981")*,[[350]](#cite_note-Trouble-356) [Jon Pareles](/wiki/Jon_Pareles "Jon Pareles") wrote:

> Decades later, what comes through these recordings above all is Mr. Dylan's unmistakable fervor, his sense of mission. The studio albums are subdued, even tentative, compared with what the songs became on the road. Mr. Dylan's voice is clear, cutting and ever improvisational; working the crowds, he was emphatic, committed, sometimes teasingly combative. And the band tears into the music.[[351]](#cite_note-357)

*Trouble No More* includes a DVD of a film consisting of live footage of Dylan's gospel performances interspersed with sermons delivered by actor [Michael Shannon](/wiki/Michael_Shannon "Michael Shannon").

In April 2018, *The New York Times* reported that Dylan was launching Heaven's Door, a range of three [whiskeys](/wiki/Whisky "Whisky"). The *Times* described the venture as "Mr. Dylan's entry into the booming celebrity-branded spirits market, the latest career twist for an artist who has spent five decades confounding expectations".[[352]](#cite_note-NYTwhiskey-358) Dylan has been involved in the creation and marketing of the range; on September 21, 2020, Dylan resurrected *Theme Time Radio Hour* with a two-hour special with the theme of "Whiskey".[[353]](#cite_note-whisk-359) In November 2018, Dylan released *[More Blood, More Tracks](/wiki/More_Blood,_More_Tracks "More Blood, More Tracks")* as Volume 14 in the Bootleg Series. The set comprises all Dylan's recordings for *Blood On the Tracks*.[[354]](#cite_note-360)

In 2019, [Netflix](/wiki/Netflix "Netflix") released *[Rolling Thunder Revue: A Bob Dylan Story by Martin Scorsese](/wiki/Rolling_Thunder_Revue:_A_Bob_Dylan_Story_by_Martin_Scorsese "Rolling Thunder Revue: A Bob Dylan Story by Martin Scorsese")*, billed as "Part documentary, part concert film, part fever dream".[[355]](#cite_note-361)[[356]](#cite_note-Willman2019-362) The film received positive reviews but aroused controversy because it mixed documentary footage filmed during the Rolling Thunder Revue in the fall of 1975 with fictitious characters and stories.[[357]](#cite_note-363)[[358]](#cite_note-364) Coinciding with the film, the box set *[The Rolling Thunder Revue: The 1975 Live Recordings](/wiki/The_Rolling_Thunder_Revue:_The_1975_Live_Recordings "The Rolling Thunder Revue: The 1975 Live Recordings")*, was released by Columbia Records. The set comprises five full Dylan performances from the tour and discovered tapes from Dylan's tour rehearsals.[[359]](#cite_note-365) The box set received an aggregate score of 89 on Metacritic, indicating "universal acclaim".[[360]](#cite_note-366) The next installment of Dylan's Bootleg Series, *[Bob Dylan (featuring Johnny Cash) – Travelin' Thru, 1967 – 1969: The Bootleg Series Vol. 15](/wiki/The_Bootleg_Series_Vol._15:_Travelin%27_Thru,_1967%E2%80%931969 "The Bootleg Series Vol. 15: Travelin' Thru, 1967–1969")*, was released on November 1. The set comprises outtakes from Dylan's albums *John Wesley Harding* and *Nashville Skyline*, and songs that Dylan recorded with Cash in Nashville in 1969 and with [Earl Scruggs](/wiki/Earl_Scruggs "Earl Scruggs") in 1970.[[361]](#cite_note-367)[[362]](#cite_note-368)

### 2020–present: *Rough and Rowdy Ways*

On March 26, 2020, Dylan released "[Murder Most Foul](/wiki/Murder_Most_Foul_(song) "Murder Most Foul (song)")", a seventeen-minute song revolving around the [Kennedy assassination](/wiki/Kennedy_assassination "Kennedy assassination"), on his YouTube channel.[[363]](#cite_note-mmf-369) *[Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)")* reported on April 8 that "Murder Most Foul" had topped the Rock Digital Song Sales Chart, the first time that Dylan had scored a number one song on a pop chart under his own name.[[364]](#cite_note-370) Three weeks later, on April 17, 2020, Dylan released another new song, "[I Contain Multitudes](/wiki/I_Contain_Multitudes "I Contain Multitudes")".[[365]](#cite_note-371)[[366]](#cite_note-372) The title is from [Walt Whitman](/wiki/Walt_Whitman "Walt Whitman")'s poem "[Song of Myself](/wiki/Song_of_Myself "Song of Myself")".[[367]](#cite_note-373) On May 7, Dylan released a third single, "[False Prophet](/wiki/False_Prophet_(song) "False Prophet (song)")", accompanied by the news that the three songs would all appear on a forthcoming double album.

*[Rough and Rowdy Ways](/wiki/Rough_and_Rowdy_Ways "Rough and Rowdy Ways")*, Dylan's 39th studio album and his first album of original material since 2012, was released on June 19 to favorable reviews.[[368]](#cite_note-metaR-374) [Alexis Petridis](/wiki/Alexis_Petridis "Alexis Petridis") wrote: "For all its bleakness, *Rough and Rowdy Ways* might well be Bob Dylan's most consistently brilliant set of songs in years: the die-hards can spend months unravelling the knottier lyrics, but you don't need a PhD in Dylanology to appreciate its singular quality and power."[[369]](#cite_note-375) [Rob Sheffield](/wiki/Rob_Sheffield "Rob Sheffield") wrote: "While the world keeps trying to celebrate him as an institution, pin him down, cast him in the Nobel Prize canon, embalm his past, this drifter always keeps on making his next escape. On *Rough and Rowdy Ways*, Dylan is exploring terrain nobody else has reached before—yet he just keeps pushing on into the future".[[370]](#cite_note-376) The album earned a score of 95 on Metacritic, indicating "universal acclaim".[[368]](#cite_note-metaR-374) In its first week of release *Rough and Rowdy Ways* reached number one on the UK album chart, making Dylan "the oldest artist to score a No. 1 of new, original material".[[371]](#cite_note-377)

In December 2020, Dylan sold his entire song catalog to [Universal Music Publishing Group](/wiki/Universal_Music_Publishing_Group "Universal Music Publishing Group"),[[372]](#cite_note-378) including both the income he receives as a songwriter and his control of their copyright. Universal, a division of the French media conglomerate [Vivendi](/wiki/Vivendi "Vivendi"), will collect all future income from the songs.[[373]](#cite_note-nytprice-379) *The New York Times* estimated the price at more than $300 million",[[373]](#cite_note-nytprice-379) although other reports suggested the figure was closer to $400 million.[[374]](#cite_note-380)

In February 2021, Columbia Records released *1970*, a three-CD set of recordings from the *Self Portrait* and *New Morning* sessions, including the entirety of the session Dylan recorded with George Harrison on May 1, 1970.[[375]](#cite_note-1970Box-381)[[376]](#cite_note-georg-382) Dylan's 80th birthday was commemorated by a virtual conference, Dylan@80, organized by the [University of Tulsa](/wiki/University_of_Tulsa "University of Tulsa") Institute for Bob Dylan Studies.[[377]](#cite_note-383) Several new biographies and studies of Dylan were published.[[378]](#cite_note-384)[[379]](#cite_note-385)

In July 2021, livestream platform Veeps presented a 50-minute performance by Dylan, *[Shadow Kingdom: The Early Songs of Bob Dylan](/wiki/Shadow_Kingdom:_The_Early_Songs_of_Bob_Dylan "Shadow Kingdom: The Early Songs of Bob Dylan")*.[[380]](#cite_note-warrior-386) Filmed in black and white with a [film noir](/wiki/Film_noir "Film noir") look,[[381]](#cite_note-lynch-387) Dylan performed 13 songs in a club setting with an audience.[[380]](#cite_note-warrior-386)[[382]](#cite_note-joy-388) The performance was favorably reviewed,[[382]](#cite_note-joy-388)[[381]](#cite_note-lynch-387) and one critic suggested the backing band resembled the style of the musical *[Girl from the North Country](/wiki/Girl_from_the_North_Country_(musical) "Girl from the North Country (musical)")*.[[383]](#cite_note-389) The [soundtrack to the film](/wiki/Shadow_Kingdom "Shadow Kingdom") was released on 2 LP and CD formats in June 2023.[[384]](#cite_note-390) In September, Dylan released *[Springtime in New York: The Bootleg Series Vol. 16 (1980–1985)](/wiki/The_Bootleg_Series_Vol._16:_Springtime_in_New_York_1980%E2%80%931985 "The Bootleg Series Vol. 16: Springtime in New York 1980–1985")*, issued in 2 LP, 2 CD and 5 CD formats. It comprised rehearsals, live recordings, out-takes and alternative takes from *Shot of Love*, *Infidels* and *Empire Burlesque*.[[385]](#cite_note-391) In *[The Daily Telegraph](/wiki/The_Daily_Telegraph "The Daily Telegraph")*, [Neil McCormick](/wiki/Neil_McCormick "Neil McCormick") wrote: "These bootleg sessions remind us that Dylan's worst period is still more interesting than most artists' purple patches".[[386]](#cite_note-392) *Springtime in New York* received an aggregate score of 85 on Metacritic.[[387]](#cite_note-393)

On July 7, 2022, [Christie's](/wiki/Christie%27s "Christie's"), London, auctioned a 2021 recording of Dylan singing "Blowin' in the Wind". The record was in an innovative "one of one" recording medium, branded as Ionic Original, which producer T Bone Burnett claimed "surpasses the sonic excellence and depth for which analogue sound is renowned, while at the same time boasting the durability of a digital recording."[[388]](#cite_note-394)[[389]](#cite_note-395) The recording fetched GBP £1,482,000—equivalent to $1,769,508.[[390]](#cite_note-1.8-396)[[391]](#cite_note-397) In November, Dylan published *[The Philosophy of Modern Song](/wiki/The_Philosophy_of_Modern_Song "The Philosophy of Modern Song")*, a collection of 66 essays on songs by other artists. *[The New Yorker](/wiki/The_New_Yorker "The New Yorker")* described it as "a rich, riffy, funny, and completely engaging book of essays".[[392]](#cite_note-398) Other reviewers praised the book's eclectic outlook,[[393]](#cite_note-399) while some questioned its variations in style and dearth of female songwriters.[[394]](#cite_note-400)

In January 2023, Dylan released *[The Bootleg Series Vol. 17: Fragments – Time Out of Mind Sessions (1996–1997)](/wiki/The_Bootleg_Series_Vol._17:_Fragments_%E2%80%93_Time_Out_of_Mind_Sessions_(1996%E2%80%931997) "The Bootleg Series Vol. 17: Fragments – Time Out of Mind Sessions (1996–1997)")* in multiple formats. The 5-CD version included a re-mix of the 1997 album "to sound more like how the songs came across when the musicians originally played them in the room" without the effects and processing which producer Daniel Lanois applied later; 25 previously unreleased out-takes from the studio sessions; and a disc of live performances of each song on the album performed by Dylan and his band in concert.[[395]](#cite_note-TOOM-401) On November 17, 2023, Dylan released *[The Complete Budokan 1978](/wiki/The_Complete_Budokan_1978 "The Complete Budokan 1978")*, containing the full recordings of the February 28 and March 1 Tokyo concerts from his 1978 Tour.[[396]](#cite_note-402)

On October 31, 2025, Dylan released *[The Bootleg Series Vol. 18: Through the Open Window 1956–1963](/wiki/The_Bootleg_Series_Vol._18:_Through_the_Open_Window_1956%E2%80%931963 "The Bootleg Series Vol. 18: Through the Open Window 1956–1963")*, an 8-CD collection of recordings chronicling his development from a 15 year-old high school student to his major concert at [Carnegie Hall](/wiki/Carnegie_Hall "Carnegie Hall") in October 1963. It was also released in a 2-CD highlights format.[[397]](#cite_note-403) The set included a 125 page booklet by historian [Sean Wilentz](/wiki/Sean_Wilentz "Sean Wilentz") and was awarded a score of 97 on the critical aggregator website Metacritic, indicating universal acclaim.[[398]](#cite_note-404)

## Never Ending Tour

Main article: [Never Ending Tour](/wiki/Never_Ending_Tour "Never Ending Tour")

[![Bob Dylan performing at Finsbury Park, London, June 18, 2011](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Bob_Dylan_Finsbury_Park_London_2011.jpg/330px-Bob_Dylan_Finsbury_Park_London_2011.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bob_Dylan_Finsbury_Park_London_2011.jpg)

Dylan performing at Finsbury Park, London, June 18, 2011

The Never Ending Tour commenced on June 7, 1988.[[399]](#cite_note-405) Dylan has played roughly 100 dates a year since, a heavier schedule than most performers who started in the 1960s.[[400]](#cite_note-406) By April 2019, Dylan and his band had played more than 3,000 shows,[[401]](#cite_note-407) anchored by long-time bassist [Tony Garnier](/wiki/Tony_Garnier_(musician) "Tony Garnier (musician)").[[402]](#cite_note-Milwauk21-408)

To the dismay of some of his audience,[[403]](#cite_note-409) Dylan's performances are unpredictable as he often alters his [arrangements](/wiki/Arrangements "Arrangements") and changes his vocal approach.[[404]](#cite_note-410) These variable performances have divided critics. [Richard Williams](/wiki/Richard_Williams_(journalist) "Richard Williams (journalist)") and Andy Gill argued that Dylan has found a successful way to present his rich legacy of material.[[405]](#cite_note-411)[[406]](#cite_note-412) Others have criticized his live performances for changing "the greatest lyrics ever written so that they are effectively unrecognisable", and giving so little to the audience that "it is difficult to understand what he is doing on stage at all".[[407]](#cite_note-413)

In September 2021, Dylan's touring company announced a series of tours which were billed as the "[Rough and Rowdy Ways World Wide Tour](/wiki/Rough_and_Rowdy_Ways_World_Wide_Tour "Rough and Rowdy Ways World Wide Tour")". The Rough and Rowdy Ways World Tour replaced Dylan's varied set lists with a more stable repertory, performing nine of the ten songs on his [2020 album](/wiki/Rough_and_Rowdy_Ways "Rough and Rowdy Ways").[[408]](#cite_note-414) Nevertheless, the tour has been referred to by the media as an extension of his ongoing Never Ending Tour.[[409]](#cite_note-415)

In 2025, Dylan completed a European leg of his Rough and Rowdy Ways world tour, starting on October 16 in Helsinki and ending in Dublin on November 25.[[410]](#cite_note-416) In December, Dylan announced dates of the 2026 edition of his “Rough and Rowdy Ways” tour with 27 U.S. concerts, commencing in Omaha, Nebraska, on March 21 and finishing in Abilene, Texas, on May 1.[[411]](#cite_note-417)

Alex Ross has summarised Dylan's touring career: "his shows cause his songs to mutate, so that no definitive or ideal version exists. Dylan's legacy will be the sum of thousands of performances, over many decades ... Every night, whether he's in good or bad form, he says, in effect, 'Think again.'"[[247]](#cite_note-Ross-253)

## Personal life

Dylan owns property around the world. He is believed to live primarily in [Point Dume](/wiki/Point_Dume "Point Dume"), on the coast of [Malibu, California](/wiki/Malibu,_California "Malibu, California").[[412]](#cite_note-418)[[413]](#cite_note-Marty-419)

### Romantic relationships

#### Echo Helstrom

[Echo Helstrom](/wiki/Echo_Helstrom "Echo Helstrom") was Dylan's high school girlfriend. The couple listened together to rhythm-and-blues on the radio, and her family exposed him to singers such as [Jimmie Rodgers](/wiki/Jimmie_Rodgers "Jimmie Rodgers") on [78 RPM records](/wiki/Phonograph_record#78_rpm_disc_developments "Phonograph record"), and a range of folk music magazines, sheet music, and manuscripts.[[414]](#cite_note-420) Helstrom is believed by some to be the inspiration for Dylan's song "[Girl from the North Country](/wiki/Girl_from_the_North_Country "Girl from the North Country")", though this is disputed.[[415]](#cite_note-421)

#### Suze Rotolo

Dylan's first serious relationship was with artist [Suze Rotolo](/wiki/Suze_Rotolo "Suze Rotolo"), a daughter of [Communist Party USA](/wiki/Communist_Party_USA "Communist Party USA") radicals. According to Dylan, "She was the most erotic thing I'd ever seen ... The air was suddenly filled with banana leaves. We started talking and my head started to spin".[[416]](#cite_note-422) Rotolo was photographed arm-in-arm with Dylan on the cover of his album *[The Freewheelin' Bob Dylan](/wiki/The_Freewheelin%27_Bob_Dylan "The Freewheelin' Bob Dylan")*. Critics have connected Rotolo to some of Dylan's early love songs, including "[Don't Think Twice It's All Right](/wiki/Don%27t_Think_Twice_It%27s_All_Right "Don't Think Twice It's All Right")". The relationship ended in 1964.[[417]](#cite_note-423) In 2008, Rotolo published a memoir about her life in Greenwich Village and relationship with Dylan in the 1960s, *A Freewheelin' Time*.[[418]](#cite_note-424)

#### Joan Baez

When [Joan Baez](/wiki/Joan_Baez "Joan Baez") met Dylan in April 1961, she had already released her [first album](/wiki/Joan_Baez_(album) "Joan Baez (album)") and was acclaimed as the "Queen of Folk".[[419]](#cite_note-425) On hearing Dylan perform his song "[With God on Our Side](/wiki/With_God_on_Our_Side_(song) "With God on Our Side (song)")", Baez later said, "I never thought anything so powerful could come out of that little toad".[[420]](#cite_note-426) In July 1963, Baez invited Dylan to join her on stage at the Newport Folk Festival, setting the scene for similar duets over the next two years.[[421]](#cite_note-GrayBaez-427) By the time of Dylan's 1965 tour of the UK, their romantic relationship had begun to fizzle out, as captured in D. A. Pennebaker's documentary film *[Dont Look Back](/wiki/Dont_Look_Back "Dont Look Back")*.[[421]](#cite_note-GrayBaez-427) Baez later toured with Dylan as a performer on his Rolling Thunder Revue in 1975–76. Baez also starred as "The Woman In White" in the film *Renaldo and Clara* (1978), directed by Dylan.[[422]](#cite_note-428) Dylan and Baez toured together again in 1984 with [Carlos Santana](/wiki/Carlos_Santana "Carlos Santana").[[421]](#cite_note-GrayBaez-427)

Baez recalled her relationship with Dylan in [Martin Scorsese](/wiki/Martin_Scorsese "Martin Scorsese")'s documentary film *No Direction Home* (2005). Baez wrote about Dylan in two autobiographies—admiringly in *Daybreak* (1968), and less admiringly in *And A Voice to Sing With* (1987). Her song "[Diamonds & Rust](/wiki/Diamonds_%26_Rust_(song) "Diamonds & Rust (song)")" has been described as "an acute portrait" of Dylan.[[421]](#cite_note-GrayBaez-427)

#### Sara Lownds

Dylan married [Sara Lownds](/wiki/Sara_Lownds "Sara Lownds"), who had worked as a model and secretary at [Drew Associates](/wiki/Drew_Associates "Drew Associates"), on November 22, 1965.[[423]](#cite_note-Gray198-429) They had four children: [Jesse Byron](/wiki/Jesse_Dylan "Jesse Dylan") (born January 6, 1966), Anna Lea (born July 11, 1967), Samuel Isaac Abram (born July 30, 1968), and [Jakob Luke](/wiki/Jakob_Dylan "Jakob Dylan") (born December 9, 1969). Dylan also adopted Sara's daughter from a prior marriage, Maria Lownds (later Dylan, born October 21, 1961). Sara Dylan played the role of Clara in Dylan's film *Renaldo and Clara* (1978). Bob and Sara Dylan were divorced on June 29, 1977.[[423]](#cite_note-Gray198-429)

#### Carolyn Dennis

Dylan and his backing singer [Carolyn Dennis](/wiki/Carolyn_Dennis "Carolyn Dennis") (often professionally known as Carol Dennis) have a daughter, Desiree Gabrielle Dennis-Dylan, born on January 31, 1986.[[424]](#cite_note-430) They were married on June 4, 1986, and divorced in October 1992. Their marriage and child remained a closely guarded secret until the publication of [Howard Sounes](/wiki/Howard_Sounes "Howard Sounes")'s biography *Down the Highway: The Life of Bob Dylan*, in 2001.[[425]](#cite_note-431)

### Religious beliefs

Growing up in Hibbing, Minnesota, Dylan and his family were part of the area's small, close-knit Jewish community, and Dylan had his *[bar mitzvah](/wiki/Bar_mitzvah "Bar mitzvah")* in May 1954.[[426]](#cite_note-432)[[15]](#cite_note-Haaretz-15) Around the time of his 30th birthday, in 1971, Dylan visited Israel, and also met Rabbi [Meir Kahane](/wiki/Meir_Kahane "Meir Kahane"), founder of the New York-based [Jewish Defense League](/wiki/Jewish_Defense_League "Jewish Defense League").[[427]](#cite_note-433)

In the late 1970s, Dylan converted to Christianity. In November 1978, guided by his friend Mary Alice Artes, Dylan made contact with the [Vineyard School of Discipleship](/wiki/Vineyard_Christian_Fellowship "Vineyard Christian Fellowship").[[195]](#cite_note-Vineyard-201) Vineyard Pastor Kenn Gulliksen recalled: "Larry Myers and Paul Emond went over to Bob's house and ministered to him. He responded by saying yes, he did in fact want Christ in his life. And he prayed that day and received the Lord".[[428]](#cite_note-434)[[429]](#cite_note-435) From January to March 1979, Dylan attended Vineyard's Bible study classes in [Reseda, California](/wiki/Reseda,_California "Reseda, California").[[195]](#cite_note-Vineyard-201)[[430]](#cite_note-436)

By 1984, Dylan was distancing himself from the "[born again](/wiki/Born_again "Born again")" label. He told [Kurt Loder](/wiki/Kurt_Loder "Kurt Loder") of *Rolling Stone*: "I've never said I'm 'born again'. That's just a media term. I don't think I've been an agnostic. I've always thought there's a superior power, that this is not the real world and that there's a world to come."[[431]](#cite_note-437) In 1997, he told [David Gates](/wiki/David_Gates_(author) "David Gates (author)") of *[Newsweek](/wiki/Newsweek "Newsweek")*:

> Here's the thing with me and the religious thing. This is the flat-out truth: I find the religiosity and philosophy in the music. I don't find it anywhere else. Songs like "Let Me Rest on a Peaceful Mountain" or "[I Saw the Light](/wiki/I_Saw_the_Light_(Hank_Williams_song) "I Saw the Light (Hank Williams song)")"—that's my religion. I don't adhere to rabbis, preachers, evangelists, all of that. I've learned more from the songs than I've learned from any of this kind of entity. The songs are my lexicon. I believe the songs.[[432]](#cite_note-438)

Dylan has supported the [Chabad Lubavitch](/wiki/Chabad_Lubavitch "Chabad Lubavitch") movement,[[433]](#cite_note-439) and has privately participated in Jewish religious events, including his sons' *bar mitzvahs* and services at [Hadar Hatorah](/wiki/Hadar_Hatorah "Hadar Hatorah"), a [Chabad Lubavitch](/wiki/Chabad_Lubavitch "Chabad Lubavitch") [yeshiva](/wiki/Yeshiva "Yeshiva"). In 1989 and 1991, he appeared on the Chabad [telethon](/wiki/Telethon "Telethon").[[434]](#cite_note-440)

Dylan has continued to perform songs from his gospel albums in concert, occasionally covering traditional religious songs. He has made passing references to his religious faith, such as in a 2004 interview with *[60 Minutes](/wiki/60_Minutes "60 Minutes")*, when he told [Ed Bradley](/wiki/Ed_Bradley "Ed Bradley"), "the only person you have to think twice about lying to is either yourself or to God". He explained his constant touring schedule as part of a bargain he made a long time ago with the "chief commander—in this earth and in the world we can't see".[[34]](#cite_note-60minutes2005-35)

Speaking to Jeff Slate of *[The Wall Street Journal](/wiki/The_Wall_Street_Journal "The Wall Street Journal")* in December 2022, Dylan reaffirmed his religious outlook: "I read the scriptures a lot, meditate and pray, light candles in church. I believe in damnation and salvation as well as [predestination](/wiki/Predestination "Predestination"). The [Five Books of Moses](/wiki/Five_Books_of_Moses "Five Books of Moses"), [Pauline Epistles](/wiki/Pauline_Epistles "Pauline Epistles"), [Invocation of the Saints](/wiki/Intercession_of_saints "Intercession of saints"), all of it."[[435]](#cite_note-441)[[436]](#cite_note-442)

## Accolades and honors

Main article: [List of awards and nominations received by Bob Dylan](/wiki/List_of_awards_and_nominations_received_by_Bob_Dylan "List of awards and nominations received by Bob Dylan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/President_Barack_Obama_presents_American_musician_Bob_Dylan_with_a_Medal_of_Freedom.jpg/250px-President_Barack_Obama_presents_American_musician_Bob_Dylan_with_a_Medal_of_Freedom.jpg)](/wiki/File:President_Barack_Obama_presents_American_musician_Bob_Dylan_with_a_Medal_of_Freedom.jpg)

President Obama presents Dylan with a Medal of Freedom, May 201.

Dylan has been inducted into the [Rock and Roll Hall of Fame](/wiki/Rock_and_Roll_Hall_of_Fame "Rock and Roll Hall of Fame"), [Nashville Songwriters Hall of Fame](/wiki/Nashville_Songwriters_Hall_of_Fame "Nashville Songwriters Hall of Fame") and [Songwriters Hall of Fame](/wiki/Songwriters_Hall_of_Fame "Songwriters Hall of Fame"). In 1997, US President [Bill Clinton](/wiki/Bill_Clinton "Bill Clinton") presented Dylan with a [Kennedy Center](/wiki/Kennedy_Center "Kennedy Center") Honor in the East Room of the [White House](/wiki/White_House "White House"), saying: "He probably had more impact on people of my generation than any other creative artist. His voice and lyrics haven't always been easy on the ear, but throughout his career Bob Dylan has never aimed to please. He's disturbed the peace and discomforted the powerful".[[437]](#cite_note-443) In May 2000, Dylan received the [Polar Music Prize](/wiki/Polar_Music_Prize "Polar Music Prize") from Sweden's [King Carl XVI](/wiki/King_Carl_XVI "King Carl XVI").[[438]](#cite_note-444) In June 2007, Dylan received the [Prince of Asturias Award](/wiki/Prince_of_Asturias_Award "Prince of Asturias Award") in the Arts category; the jury called him "a living myth in the history of popular music and a light for a generation that dreamed of changing the world."[[4]](#cite_note-EncBr-4)[[439]](#cite_note-445) In 2008, the [Pulitzer Prize](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize "Pulitzer Prize") jury awarded him a special citation for "his profound impact on popular music and American culture, marked by lyrical compositions of extraordinary poetic power".[[440]](#cite_note-446)

Dylan received the [Presidential Medal of Freedom](/wiki/Presidential_Medal_of_Freedom "Presidential Medal of Freedom") in May 2012.[[441]](#cite_note-Medal-447)[[442]](#cite_note-448) President [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama"), presenting Dylan with the award, said "There is not a bigger giant in the history of American music." Obama praised Dylan's voice for its "unique gravelly power that redefined not just what music sounded like but the message it carried and how it made people feel".[[443]](#cite_note-449) In November 2013, Dylan was awarded France's highest honor, the [Légion d'Honneur](/wiki/L%C3%A9gion_d%27Honneur "Légion d'Honneur"),[[444]](#cite_note-Legiond'H-450) despite the misgiving of the grand chancellor of the Légion, who had declared him unworthy.[[444]](#cite_note-Legiond'H-450) In February 2015, Dylan accepted the [MusiCares Person of the Year](/wiki/MusiCares_Person_of_the_Year "MusiCares Person of the Year") award from the [National Academy of Recording Arts and Sciences](/wiki/National_Academy_of_Recording_Arts_and_Sciences "National Academy of Recording Arts and Sciences"), in recognition of his philanthropic and artistic contributions.[[445]](#cite_note-451)

### Nobel Prize in Literature

Main article: [2016 Nobel Prize in Literature](/wiki/2016_Nobel_Prize_in_Literature "2016 Nobel Prize in Literature")

[Sara Danius](/wiki/Sara_Danius "Sara Danius") announces the Nobel Prize in Literature 2016

In 1996, Gordon Ball of the [Virginia Military Institute](/wiki/Virginia_Military_Institute "Virginia Military Institute") nominated Dylan for the [Nobel Prize in Literature](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Literature "Nobel Prize in Literature"),[[446]](#cite_note-452)[[447]](#cite_note-453) initiating a campaign that lasted for 20 years.[[448]](#cite_note-454) On October 13, 2016, the Nobel committee announced that it would award Dylan the prize "for having created new poetic expressions within the great American song tradition".[[6]](#cite_note-NYTNobel-6) [Horace Engdahl](/wiki/Horace_Engdahl "Horace Engdahl"), a member of the Nobel Committee, described Dylan as "a singer worthy of a place beside the Greek bards, beside [Ovid](/wiki/Ovid "Ovid"), beside the [Romantic visionaries](/wiki/Romantic_poetry "Romantic poetry"), beside the kings and queens of [the blues](/wiki/The_blues "The blues"), beside the forgotten masters of brilliant [standards](/wiki/Standard_(music) "Standard (music)")".[[449]](#cite_note-455) *The New York Times* reported: "Mr. Dylan, 75, is the first musician to win the award, and his selection was perhaps the most radical choice in a history stretching back to 1901."[[6]](#cite_note-NYTNobel-6) Dylan remained silent for days after receiving the award,[[450]](#cite_note-456) and then told journalist [Edna Gundersen](/wiki/Edna_Gundersen "Edna Gundersen") that it was "amazing, incredible. Whoever dreams about something like that?"[[451]](#cite_note-Gundersen-457) Dylan's Nobel lecture was posted on the Nobel Prize website on June 5, 2017.[[452]](#cite_note-458) [Andrea Pitzer](/wiki/Andrea_Pitzer "Andrea Pitzer") suggested in *[Slate](/wiki/Slate_(magazine) "Slate (magazine)")* magazine that Dylan may have taken some references to *[Moby-Dick](/wiki/Moby-Dick "Moby-Dick")* in his Nobel lecture from [SparkNotes](/wiki/SparkNotes "SparkNotes").[[453]](#cite_note-459)

## Legacy

Dylan has been described as one of the most influential cultural figures of the 20th century. He was included in the [*Time* 100: The Most Important People of the Century](/wiki/Time_100:_The_Most_Important_People_of_the_Century "Time 100: The Most Important People of the Century"), where he was called "master poet, caustic social critic and intrepid, guiding spirit of the counterculture generation".[[454]](#cite_note-Time100Dylan-460) [Paul Simon](/wiki/Paul_Simon "Paul Simon") suggested that Dylan's early compositions virtually took over the folk genre: "[Dylan's] early songs were very rich ... with strong melodies. 'Blowin' in the Wind' has a really strong melody. He so enlarged himself through the folk background that he incorporated it for a while. He defined the genre for a while."[[455]](#cite_note-461)

For many critics, Dylan's greatest achievement was the cultural synthesis exemplified by his mid-1960s trilogy of albums—*Bringing It All Back Home*, *Highway 61 Revisited* and *Blonde on Blonde*. In [Mike Marqusee](/wiki/Mike_Marqusee "Mike Marqusee")'s words:

> Between late 1964 and the middle of 1966, Dylan created a body of work that remains unique. Drawing on folk, blues, country, R&B, rock'n'roll, gospel, [British beat](/wiki/British_beat "British beat"), [symbolist](/wiki/Symbolist "Symbolist"), [modernist](/wiki/Modernist_poetry_in_English "Modernist poetry in English") and [Beat](/wiki/Beat_poetry "Beat poetry") poetry, [surrealism](/wiki/Surrealism "Surrealism") and [Dada](/wiki/Dada "Dada"), advertising jargon and social commentary, [Fellini](/wiki/Fellini "Fellini") and [*Mad* magazine](/wiki/Mad_magazine "Mad magazine"), he forged a coherent and original artistic voice and vision. The beauty of these albums retains the power to shock and console.[[456]](#cite_note-Marqusee139-462)

[Michael Gray](/wiki/Michael_Gray_(author) "Michael Gray (author)") initiated serious critical scrutiny when he published *Song & Dance Man: The Art of Bob Dylan* in 1972, situating Dylan's work in the context of the English literary tradition, [Donne](/wiki/John_Donne "John Donne"), [Browning](/wiki/Robert_Browning "Robert Browning") and [T. S. Eliot](/wiki/T._S._Eliot "T. S. Eliot").[[457]](#cite_note-463) Also in 1972, [Craig McGregor](/wiki/Craig_McGregor "Craig McGregor") published *Bob Dylan: A Retrospective*, an anthology containing critical essays about Dylan by [Robert Shelton](/wiki/Robert_Shelton_(critic) "Robert Shelton (critic)"), [Nat Hentoff](/wiki/Nat_Hentoff "Nat Hentoff"), [Ellen Willis](/wiki/Ellen_Willis "Ellen Willis") and other contemporary cultural critics. In 1998, [Stanford University](/wiki/Stanford_University "Stanford University") sponsored the first international academic conference on Bob Dylan held in the United States.[[458]](#cite_note-464) In 2004, [Richard F. Thomas](/wiki/Richard_F._Thomas "Richard F. Thomas"), [Classics](/wiki/Classics "Classics") professor at [Harvard University](/wiki/Harvard_University "Harvard University"), created a freshman seminar titled "Dylan", which aimed "to put the artist in context of not just popular culture of the last half-century, but the tradition of classical poets like [Virgil](/wiki/Virgil "Virgil") and [Homer](/wiki/Homer "Homer")."[[459]](#cite_note-465) Thomas went on to publish *Why Bob Dylan Matters*, exploring Dylan's connections with [Greco-Roman literature](/wiki/Classical_antiquity "Classical antiquity").[[460]](#cite_note-466)

Literary critic [Christopher Ricks](/wiki/Christopher_Ricks "Christopher Ricks") published *[Dylan's Visions of Sin](/wiki/Dylan%27s_Visions_of_Sin "Dylan's Visions of Sin")*, an appreciation of Dylan's work.[[461]](#cite_note-467) After Dylan's Nobel win, Ricks reflected: "I'd not have written a book about Dylan, to stand alongside my books on [Milton](/wiki/John_Milton "John Milton") and [Keats](/wiki/Keats "Keats"), [Tennyson](/wiki/Tennyson "Tennyson") and T.S. Eliot, if I didn't think Dylan a genius of and with language."[[462]](#cite_note-468) The critical consensus that Dylan's songwriting was his outstanding creative achievement was articulated by *[Encyclopædia Britannica](/wiki/Encyclop%C3%A6dia_Britannica "Encyclopædia Britannica")*: "Hailed as the [Shakespeare](/wiki/Shakespeare "Shakespeare") of his generation, Dylan ... set the standard for lyric writing."[[4]](#cite_note-EncBr-4) Former British [poet laureate](/wiki/Poet_laureate "Poet laureate") [Andrew Motion](/wiki/Andrew_Motion "Andrew Motion") said Dylan's lyrics should be studied in schools.[[463]](#cite_note-469) The journalist [Edna Gundersen](/wiki/Edna_Gundersen "Edna Gundersen") noted that lines such as "When you got nothing, you got nothing to lose"; "a hard rain's a-gonna fall"; "to live outside the law you must be honest" had entered the vernacular, and "you don't need a weather man to know which way the wind blows" and "the times they are a-changin'" appear in *[Bartlett's Familiar Quotations](/wiki/Bartlett%27s_Familiar_Quotations "Bartlett's Familiar Quotations")*.[[464]](#cite_note-Gund2001-470)

Dylan is considered one of the greatest songwriters of all time.[[465]](#cite_note-471)[[4]](#cite_note-EncBr-4) *Rolling Stone* ranked him first on its 2015 list of the [100 Greatest Songwriters of All Time](/wiki/Rolling_Stone%27s_100_Greatest_Songwriters_of_All_Time "Rolling Stone's 100 Greatest Songwriters of All Time"),[[466]](#cite_note-Rolling_Stone_100_Greatest_Songwriters-472) fifteenth on its 2023 list of the Greatest Singers of All Time,[[467]](#cite_note-473) and placed "[Like A Rolling Stone](/wiki/Like_A_Rolling_Stone "Like A Rolling Stone")" first on their [list of greatest songs](/wiki/Rolling_Stone%27s_500_Greatest_Songs_of_All_Time "Rolling Stone's 500 Greatest Songs of All Time") in 2004 and 2011.[[107]](#cite_note-LARS-111) He was listed second on the magazine's [list of the hundred greatest artists](/wiki/Rolling_Stone%27s_100_Greatest_Artists_of_All_Time "Rolling Stone's 100 Greatest Artists of All Time").[[468]](#cite_note-474) The *Rolling Stone Encyclopedia of Rock & Roll* writes that "His lyrics—the first in rock to be seriously regarded as literature—became so well known that politicians from [Jimmy Carter](/wiki/Jimmy_Carter "Jimmy Carter") to [Václav Havel](/wiki/V%C3%A1clav_Havel "Václav Havel") have cited them as an influence."[[236]](#cite_note-R&REncylopedia-242)

Musicians who have acknowledged Dylan's influence include [John Lennon](/wiki/John_Lennon "John Lennon"),[[469]](#cite_note-475) [Paul McCartney](/wiki/Paul_McCartney "Paul McCartney"),[[470]](#cite_note-476) [Jerry Garcia](/wiki/Jerry_Garcia "Jerry Garcia"),[[471]](#cite_note-477) [Pete Townshend](/wiki/Pete_Townshend "Pete Townshend"),[[472]](#cite_note-478) [Syd Barrett](/wiki/Syd_Barrett "Syd Barrett"),[[473]](#cite_note-479) [Tim Buckley](/wiki/Tim_Buckley "Tim Buckley"),[[474]](#cite_note-480) [Joni Mitchell](/wiki/Joni_Mitchell "Joni Mitchell"),[[475]](#cite_note-481) [Neil Young](/wiki/Neil_Young "Neil Young"),[[476]](#cite_note-482) [Bruce Springsteen](/wiki/Bruce_Springsteen "Bruce Springsteen"),[[109]](#cite_note-Springsteen-113) [David Bowie](/wiki/David_Bowie "David Bowie"),[[477]](#cite_note-483)[[464]](#cite_note-Gund2001-470) [Bryan Ferry](/wiki/Bryan_Ferry "Bryan Ferry"),[[478]](#cite_note-484) [Patti Smith](/wiki/Patti_Smith "Patti Smith"),[[479]](#cite_note-485) [Joe Strummer](/wiki/Joe_Strummer "Joe Strummer"),[[480]](#cite_note-486) [Bono](/wiki/Bono "Bono"),[[481]](#cite_note-487)[[464]](#cite_note-Gund2001-470) [Nick Cave](/wiki/Nick_Cave "Nick Cave"),[[482]](#cite_note-488) [Leonard Cohen](/wiki/Leonard_Cohen "Leonard Cohen"),[[483]](#cite_note-489) [Tom Waits](/wiki/Tom_Waits "Tom Waits")[[484]](#cite_note-490) and [Chuck D](/wiki/Chuck_D "Chuck D").[[485]](#cite_note-491)[[464]](#cite_note-Gund2001-470) Dylan significantly contributed to the initial success of both [the Byrds](/wiki/The_Byrds "The Byrds") and [the Band](/wiki/The_Band "The Band"): the Byrds achieved chart success with their version of "[Mr. Tambourine Man](/wiki/Mr._Tambourine_Man#The_Byrds'_version "Mr. Tambourine Man")" and their 1965 album *[Mr. Tambourine Man](/wiki/Mr._Tambourine_Man_(album) "Mr. Tambourine Man (album)")*, while the Band were Dylan's backing band [on his 1966 tour](/wiki/Bob_Dylan_World_Tour_1966 "Bob Dylan World Tour 1966"), recorded *The Basement Tapes* with him in 1967[[486]](#cite_note-492) and featured three previously unreleased Dylan songs on their [debut album](/wiki/Music_From_Big_Pink "Music From Big Pink").[[487]](#cite_note-493) [Johnny Cash](/wiki/Johnny_Cash "Johnny Cash"), introducing "Wanted Man", said "I don't have to tell you who Bob Dylan is—the greatest writer of our time."[[488]](#cite_note-494)

Some critics have dissented from the view of Dylan as a visionary figure in popular music. In his book *Awopbopaloobop Alopbamboom*, [Nik Cohn](/wiki/Nik_Cohn "Nik Cohn") objected: "I can't take the vision of Dylan as seer, as teenage messiah, as everything else he's been worshipped as. The way I see him, he's a minor talent with a major gift for self-hype".[[489]](#cite_note-495) Australian critic [Jack Marx](/wiki/Jack_Marx "Jack Marx") credited Dylan with changing the persona of the rock star: "What cannot be disputed is that Dylan invented the arrogant, faux-cerebral posturing that has been the dominant style in rock since, with everyone from [Mick Jagger](/wiki/Mick_Jagger "Mick Jagger") to [Eminem](/wiki/Eminem "Eminem") educating themselves from the Dylan handbook".[[490]](#cite_note-496)

Fellow musicians have also expressed critical views. Joni Mitchell described Dylan as a "plagiarist" and his voice as "fake" in a 2010 interview in the *[Los Angeles Times](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times "Los Angeles Times")*.[[491]](#cite_note-497)[[492]](#cite_note-498)[[493]](#cite_note-499) Mitchell's comments led to discussions on Dylan's use of other people's material, both supporting and criticizing him.[[494]](#cite_note-500) Talking to [Mikal Gilmore](/wiki/Mikal_Gilmore "Mikal Gilmore") in *Rolling Stone* in 2012, Dylan responded to the allegation of plagiarism, including his use of Henry Timrod's verse in his album *Modern Times*,[[281]](#cite_note-nytTimrod-287) by saying that it was "part of the tradition".[[495]](#cite_note-501)[[a 7]](#cite_note-502)

In 2007, [Todd Haynes](/wiki/Todd_Haynes "Todd Haynes") released the film *[I'm Not There](/wiki/I%27m_Not_There "I'm Not There")*, "inspired by the music and many lives of Bob Dylan".[[496]](#cite_note-Variety-07-503) The movie used six actors, [Christian Bale](/wiki/Christian_Bale "Christian Bale"), [Cate Blanchett](/wiki/Cate_Blanchett "Cate Blanchett"), [Marcus Carl Franklin](/wiki/Marcus_Carl_Franklin "Marcus Carl Franklin"), [Richard Gere](/wiki/Richard_Gere "Richard Gere"), [Heath Ledger](/wiki/Heath_Ledger "Heath Ledger") and [Ben Whishaw](/wiki/Ben_Whishaw "Ben Whishaw"), to explore different facets of Dylan's life.[[496]](#cite_note-Variety-07-503)[[497]](#cite_note-504) Irish playwright [Conor McPherson](/wiki/Conor_McPherson "Conor McPherson") wrote and directed the musical [*Girl from the North Country*](/wiki/Girl_from_the_North_Country_(musical) "Girl from the North Country (musical)"), which used Dylan's songs to tell the stories of characters during the [Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") years, set in Dylan's birthplace, [Duluth, Minnesota](/wiki/Duluth,_Minnesota "Duluth, Minnesota"). The play premiered in London in 2017.[[498]](#cite_note-505)[[499]](#cite_note-506) Dylan's rise to stardom, from his arrival in New York in 1961 to his [controversial performance at Newport in 1965](/wiki/Electric_Dylan_controversy "Electric Dylan controversy"), was portrayed by the feature film *[A Complete Unknown](/wiki/A_Complete_Unknown "A Complete Unknown")*, which opened in the U.S. on December 25, 2024.[[500]](#cite_note-507)[[501]](#cite_note-508) The film garnered generally favorable reviews, with praise for actor [Timothée Chalamet](/wiki/Timoth%C3%A9e_Chalamet "Timothée Chalamet") as Dylan.[[502]](#cite_note-509)

If Dylan's work in the 1960s was seen as bringing intellectual ambition to popular music,[[456]](#cite_note-Marqusee139-462) critics in the 21st century described him as a figure who had greatly expanded the folk culture from which he initially emerged. In his review of *I'm Not There*, [J. Hoberman](/wiki/J._Hoberman "J. Hoberman") wrote:

> Elvis might never have been born, but someone else would surely have brought the world rock 'n' roll. No such logic accounts for Bob Dylan. No iron law of history demanded that a would-be Elvis from Hibbing, Minnesota, would swerve through the Greenwich Village folk revival to become the world's first and greatest rock 'n' roll beatnik bard and then—having achieved fame and adoration beyond reckoning—vanish into a folk tradition of his own making.[[503]](#cite_note-510)

## Archives and recognition

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/Bob_Dylan_Drive_street_sign_in_Hibbing%2C_Minnesota.jpg/250px-Bob_Dylan_Drive_street_sign_in_Hibbing%2C_Minnesota.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bob_Dylan_Drive_street_sign_in_Hibbing,_Minnesota.jpg)

Bob Dylan Drive street sign in Hibbing, Minnesota

The sale of [Dylan's archive](/wiki/The_Bob_Dylan_Archive "The Bob Dylan Archive") of about 6,000 notebooks, drafts of lyrics, recordings, and correspondence to the [George Kaiser Family Foundation](/wiki/George_Kaiser "George Kaiser") and the [University of Tulsa](/wiki/University_of_Tulsa "University of Tulsa") was announced in March 2016. The sale price was "an estimated $15 million to $20 million".[[504]](#cite_note-SisarioNYT-511)[[505]](#cite_note-512) To house the archive, the [Bob Dylan Center](/wiki/Bob_Dylan_Center "Bob Dylan Center") in [Tulsa, Oklahoma](/wiki/Tulsa,_Oklahoma "Tulsa, Oklahoma") opened on May 10, 2022.[[506]](#cite_note-513)[[507]](#cite_note-514)

In 2005, 7th Avenue East in Hibbing, Minnesota, where Dylan lived from ages 6 to 18, received the honorary name Bob Dylan Drive.[[508]](#cite_note-515)[[509]](#cite_note-516) In 2006, the 1.8 miles (2.9 km) Bob Dylan Way cultural path was inaugurated in Duluth, Minnesota; it links "cultural and historically significant areas of downtown for the tourists".[[510]](#cite_note-517)

In 2015, a 160-foot-wide Dylan mural by Brazilian street artist [Eduardo Kobra](/wiki/Eduardo_Kobra "Eduardo Kobra") was unveiled in downtown Minneapolis.[[511]](#cite_note-518)

In December 2013, the [Fender Stratocaster](/wiki/Fender_Stratocaster "Fender Stratocaster") which Dylan had played at the [1965 Newport Folk Festival](/wiki/Electric_Dylan_controversy "Electric Dylan controversy") fetched $965,000, the second highest price paid for a guitar.[[512]](#cite_note-519) In June 2014, Dylan's hand-written lyrics of "Like a Rolling Stone" fetched $2 million at auction, a record for a popular music manuscript.[[513]](#cite_note-520)[[514]](#cite_note-521)

## Visual arts

### Painting

Dylan's visual art was first seen by the public via a painting he contributed for the cover of [the Band](/wiki/The_Band "The Band")'s *[Music from Big Pink](/wiki/Music_from_Big_Pink "Music from Big Pink")* album in 1968.[[515]](#cite_note-522) The cover of Dylan's own 1970 album *[Self Portrait](/wiki/Self_Portrait_(Bob_Dylan_album) "Self Portrait (Bob Dylan album)")* features the painting of a human face by Dylan.[[516]](#cite_note-523) More of Dylan's artwork was revealed with the 1973 publication of his book *[Writings and Drawings](/wiki/Writings_and_Drawings "Writings and Drawings")*.[[517]](#cite_note-524) The cover of Dylan's 1974 album *[Planet Waves](/wiki/Planet_Waves "Planet Waves")* again featured one of his paintings. In 1994 [Random House](/wiki/Random_House "Random House") published *Drawn Blank*, a book of Dylan's drawings.[[518]](#cite_note-525) In 2007, the first public exhibition of Dylan's paintings, *The Drawn Blank Series*, opened at the Kunstsammlungen in [Chemnitz](/wiki/Chemnitz "Chemnitz"), Germany;[[519]](#cite_note-EncyclopediaDrawn-526) it showcased more than 200 watercolors and [gouaches](/wiki/Gouache "Gouache") made from the original drawings. The exhibition coincided with the publication of *Bob Dylan: The Drawn Blank Series*, which includes 170 reproductions from the series.[[519]](#cite_note-EncyclopediaDrawn-526)[[520]](#cite_note-NYTimesArtist-527) From September 2010 until April 2011, the [National Gallery of Denmark](/wiki/National_Gallery_of_Denmark "National Gallery of Denmark") exhibited 40 large-scale acrylic paintings by Dylan, *The Brazil Series*.[[521]](#cite_note-528)

In July 2011, a leading contemporary art gallery, [Gagosian Gallery](/wiki/Gagosian_Gallery "Gagosian Gallery"), announced their representation of Dylan's paintings.[[522]](#cite_note-529) An exhibition of Dylan's art, *The Asia Series*, opened at the Gagosian Madison Avenue Gallery on September 20, displaying Dylan's paintings of scenes in China and the Far East.[[523]](#cite_note-530) *The New York Times* reported that "some fans and Dylanologists have raised questions about whether some of these paintings are based on the singer's own experiences and observations, or on photographs that are widely available and were not taken by Mr. Dylan". *The Times* pointed to close resemblances between Dylan's paintings and historic photos of Japan and China, and photos taken by [Dmitri Kessel](/wiki/Dmitri_Kessel "Dmitri Kessel") and [Henri Cartier-Bresson](/wiki/Henri_Cartier-Bresson "Henri Cartier-Bresson").[[524]](#cite_note-531) Art critic [Blake Gopnik](/wiki/Blake_Gopnik "Blake Gopnik") has defended Dylan's artistic practice, arguing: "Ever since the birth of photography, painters have used it as the basis for their works: [Edgar Degas](/wiki/Edgar_Degas "Edgar Degas") and [Édouard Vuillard](/wiki/%C3%89douard_Vuillard "Édouard Vuillard") and other favorite artists—even [Edvard Munch](/wiki/Edvard_Munch "Edvard Munch")—all took or used photos as sources for their art, sometimes barely altering them".[[525]](#cite_note-532) The [Magnum photo agency](/wiki/Magnum_Photos "Magnum Photos") confirmed that Dylan had licensed the reproduction rights of these photographs.[[526]](#cite_note-533)

Dylan's second show at the Gagosian Gallery, *Revisionist Art*, opened in November 2012. The show consisted of thirty paintings, transforming and satirizing popular magazines, including *[Playboy](/wiki/Playboy "Playboy")* and *[Babytalk](/wiki/Babytalk_(magazine) "Babytalk (magazine)")*.[[527]](#cite_note-534)[[528]](#cite_note-535) In February 2013, Dylan exhibited the *New Orleans Series* of paintings at the [Palazzo Reale](/wiki/Royal_Palace_of_Milan "Royal Palace of Milan") in Milan.[[529]](#cite_note-536) In August 2013, Britain's [National Portrait Gallery, London](/wiki/National_Portrait_Gallery,_London "National Portrait Gallery, London") hosted Dylan's first major UK exhibition, *Face Value*, featuring twelve pastel portraits.[[530]](#cite_note-537)

In November 2016, the Halcyon Gallery featured a collection of artworks by Dylan. The exhibition, *The Beaten Path*, depicted American landscapes and urban scenes, inspired by Dylan's travels across the US.[[531]](#cite_note-538) The show was reviewed by *[Vanity Fair](/wiki/Vanity_Fair_(magazine) "Vanity Fair (magazine)")* and *[Asia Times Online](/wiki/Asia_Times_Online "Asia Times Online")*.[[532]](#cite_note-539)[[533]](#cite_note-540)[[534]](#cite_note-541) In October 2018, the Halcyon Gallery mounted an exhibition of Dylan's drawings, *Mondo Scripto*. The works consisted of Dylan hand-written lyrics of his songs, with each song illustrated by a drawing.[[535]](#cite_note-542)
